title: OPEN_WMS_ASN_STATUSES counts CREATE_PENDING / CREATE_IN_FLIGHT as firm incoming stock — can indefinitely defer archive (PR #156)
description:
PR #156's `lib/domain/inventory/product-lifecycle-archive.ts`:

```ts
const OPEN_WMS_ASN_STATUSES = ['CREATE_PENDING', 'CREATE_IN_FLIGHT', 'OPEN', 'PARTIALLY_BOOKED_IN'] as const
```

`CREATE_PENDING` and `CREATE_IN_FLIGHT` are pre-confirmation states — they represent ASNs IMS *intends* to create on a WMS but hasn't successfully created yet. Possible outcomes:
1. ASN creation succeeds → status moves to OPEN → eventually PARTIALLY_BOOKED_IN → BOOKED_IN
2. ASN creation fails repeatedly (network, WMS rejects, dead-letter) → status stays CREATE_PENDING / CREATE_IN_FLIGHT indefinitely until manually intervened

For case (2), the auto-archive job treats the unverified ASN as 'incoming stock', skipping archive forever. A product with stale CREATE_PENDING ASNs that will never actually deliver remains in EOL status indefinitely.

**Concrete failure mode:**

- Product P, lifecycle EOL, stock=0 everywhere
- An ASN was created for P some time ago, status=CREATE_PENDING, but the WMS rejected it
- The retry has dead-lettered (queue policy: stop after N failures)
- Auto-archive job: `getProductIncomingStock(P).wmsAsn > 0` → skipWithIncoming
- P stays in EOL forever; operators wonder why archive isn't firing
- The skippedWithIncoming counter increments every cron tick

**Why this matters:**

The pre-confirmation states represent INTENT, not commitment. Counting them as firm incoming stock conservatively defers archive, but the failure mode is silent and persistent.

**Fix options:**

1. **Exclude pre-confirmation states** from the incoming-stock check:
   ```ts
   const OPEN_WMS_ASN_STATUSES = ['OPEN', 'PARTIALLY_BOOKED_IN'] as const
   ```
   
2. **Time-bound the pre-confirmation states**: count them as incoming only if created in the last N days; otherwise treat as dead.

3. **Surface diagnostic**: when archive is skipped due to incoming WMS ASN, include the ASN status in the cron result so operators can spot stuck ASNs.

Option 1 is the cleanest; if a CREATE_PENDING ASN does eventually succeed and the product becomes non-archivable, the lifecycle could be manually un-archived. The risk is small because EOL products typically don't have pending ASNs being created.

**Test gap:**

The existing tests for the archive helper don't cover the CREATE_PENDING-only case — would catch this regression.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decide policy: exclude CREATE_PENDING / CREATE_IN_FLIGHT from OPEN_WMS_ASN_STATUSES, OR add a time-bound, OR surface stuck-ASN diagnostic
- Add a test fixture: product P with stock=0 AND only CREATE_PENDING ASN → assert archive behavior matches policy
- Document the OPEN_WMS_ASN_STATUSES choice in the helper file's JSDoc with the trade-off
- Audit OPEN_PO_STATUSES and OPEN_PRODUCTION_STATUSES for similar pre-confirmation states
notes: Filed during PR #156 review. Smaller scope than 27w but a tangible silent failure mode for products in EOL with stuck WMS ASNs.
close_reason: Reconciled after importing origin/codex/dead-stock-report issues.jsonl: these PR #155/#156 findings were already fixed or superseded on the current PR branch before the import reopened their Beads state.

title: getReorderReport uses SupplierProduct[0] as primary supplier, preferredSupplier only as fallback — inverts auto-update intent (PR #156)
description:
PR #156's `getReorderReport` in `lib/domain/inventory/replenishment-reports.ts`:

```ts
const supplier = product.supplierProducts[0] ?? null
const displaySupplier = supplier
  ? { id: supplier.supplierId, name: supplier.supplier.name, sku: supplier.supplierSku }
  : product.preferredSupplier
  ? { id: product.preferredSupplier.id, name: product.preferredSupplier.name, sku: null }
  : null
```

Order of precedence:
1. **`supplierProducts[0]`** (the first row from SupplierProduct catalog — many-to-many relation)
2. **`preferredSupplier`** (only when no SupplierProduct row exists)

This INVERTS the intent of the PR's preferred-supplier auto-update mechanism:

**Scenario:** Product P historically purchased from Supplier A (SupplierProduct row exists, catalog metadata: supplierSku, leadTimeDays). Recently, an operator placed a PO with Supplier B (after some agreement to switch). The PO_SENT transition fired `updatePreferredSuppliersForPlacedPurchaseOrder` → `product.preferredSupplierId = B`. So the 'latest placed PO wins' rule set B.

**But the reorder report:**
- Reads `product.supplierProducts[0]` = A (still the first catalog row)
- displaySupplier = A
- The report's supplier filter, draft-PO generation, lead-time computation, etc. all use A
- The operator's recent decision to use B is silently invisible in the reorder UI

The auto-update wrote the latest signal but the read path ignores it.

**Why this matters:**

P8.2's stated workflow goal: 'reorder workflow follows real buying history instead of stale manual metadata'. The current implementation keeps stale manual metadata (SupplierProduct[0]) as authoritative and treats the real buying history as advisory.

**Two possible fixes:**

1. **Invert the precedence**: prefer `product.preferredSupplier` first; fall back to `SupplierProduct` only when no preferred is set. This matches P8.2's intent.
2. **Prefer the SupplierProduct row matching preferredSupplier**: when both exist, use the SupplierProduct row for supplier=preferred (gives you both the latest-PO signal AND the catalog metadata for that supplier). This is the most useful for operators.

**Code path:**

```ts
const preferredCatalog = product.supplierProducts.find(sp => sp.supplierId === product.preferredSupplierId)
const supplier = preferredCatalog 
  ?? product.supplierProducts[0] 
  ?? null
const displaySupplier = supplier
  ? { id: supplier.supplierId, name: supplier.supplier.name, sku: supplier.supplierSku }
  : product.preferredSupplier
  ? { id: product.preferredSupplier.id, name: product.preferredSupplier.name, sku: null }
  : null
```

This delivers the catalog metadata for the most-recently-used supplier when available.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update getReorderReport to prefer the SupplierProduct row matching preferredSupplierId, falling back to supplierProducts[0]
- Add tests:
  - Product with SupplierProduct rows for A and B, preferredSupplier=B → report shows B with B's catalog metadata
  - Product with only SupplierProduct[A], preferredSupplier=A → report shows A
  - Product with SupplierProduct[A], preferredSupplier=null → report shows A (current behavior)
  - Product with no SupplierProduct rows, preferredSupplier=B → report shows B without catalog metadata
- Update the related `filters.supplierId` filter logic (currently filters by preferredSupplierId only — should it also match products where supplierProducts has the requested supplier?)
notes: Filed during PR #156 review. Closes the P8.2 implementation loop — the auto-update writes the signal, the read path needs to honor it.

title: P8.2 'latest placed PO wins' supplier rule doesn't handle emergency/backup POs or transition timing (PR #155 follow-up)
description:
PR #155 P8.2 proposes:

> Update the product supplier automatically when a purchase order is placed for that product. If a PO contains multiple products, update each included product to that PO's supplier. If a product appears on multiple POs, the latest placed PO wins.

This rule has several operational gaps:

**1. Emergency / backup supplier overrides the primary.**

Scenario: Product P is consistently sourced from Supplier A. A's stock is out for one week; ops places an emergency PO for P with Supplier B (a backup). After 'latest placed PO wins', P's supplier field is now B. The next reorder forecast → draft PO generator scopes P to supplier B, not A. Operator has to manually re-bind P to A, or A is silently demoted.

Workarounds: a 'supplier_lock' boolean per product to opt out of auto-update; or 'mark as one-off' flag on POs to skip the auto-update.

**2. Update timing: PO_SENT vs receipt status.**

The plan says 'when a purchase order is placed'. Placement is the `PO_SENT` transition. If the PO is later returned or cancelled (RETURNED, CANCELLED) for fulfillment failure, the supplier auto-update is NOT rolled back. P now points at a supplier that couldn't deliver. Should the update fire on:
   - PO_SENT (decision: 'we're committing to this supplier')
   - PARTIALLY_RECEIVED (decision: 'this supplier has actually delivered')
   - RECEIVED (decision: 'this supplier completed delivery')

Different operations teams answer this differently. Plan should pick.

**3. Mass-update side effects.**

A single PO line update transaction now triggers N product updates (one per line). For a 200-line PO, that's 200 `product` row updates in the same transaction as the PO line writes. Worth specifying:
   - Same-transaction or async via outbox?
   - Lock ordering: products by ID asc to avoid deadlock with concurrent stock movements that touch the same product rows
   - Activity-log volume: 200 'product_supplier_updated' activity-log entries per PO

**4. PO multi-supplier impossible scenarios.**

The plan says 'If a PO contains multiple products, update each included product to that PO's supplier.' A PO has exactly one supplier (PurchaseOrder.supplierId), so 'PO's supplier' is always singular. Fine. But:
   - What about a PO line for a product where SupplierProduct doesn't have a row for this supplier? Implicitly creates one, or refuses?
   - What if the new supplier's currency differs from the product's expected cost basis?

**5. Race with multi-PO concurrent placement.**

Two POs placed within milliseconds for the same product, different suppliers. Final value of `product.supplier` depends on transaction commit ordering. The plan says 'latest placed PO wins' but doesn't define 'placed' precisely — committed-at? sent-at? created-at?

**Why this matters:**

The auto-update is the load-bearing mechanism for reorder draft generation in P8.2. Subtle race / timing / opt-out semantics determine whether ops trusts the suggestion or treats it as 'noise that must be manually corrected after every PO'.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update P8.2 to specify:
  - When the update fires (PO_SENT vs received)
  - Whether the update is per-tx or async
  - Opt-out mechanism for one-off / emergency POs (e.g., a 'don't update product supplier' flag on the PO header)
  - Concurrency policy for multi-PO placement (which timestamp wins)
  - Rollback semantics when a PO is cancelled / returned
notes: Filed during PR #155 second-pass review. The 'latest wins' rule is simple to implement but loses signal about primary vs ad-hoc supplier choices. Worth getting ops/procurement sign-off before implementation.
close_reason: Reconciled after importing origin/codex/dead-stock-report issues.jsonl: these PR #155/#156 findings were already fixed or superseded on the current PR branch before the import reopened their Beads state.

title: P8.2 product supplier field overlaps with existing SupplierProduct many-to-many relation — spec needs to reconcile (PR #155 follow-up)
description:
PR #155 (commit 65b726da) adds P8.2 to docs/plan.md:

> 1. Add a supplier field to products, backed by a relation to the supplier/vendor model used by purchase orders.

But the codebase already has `SupplierProduct` (prisma/schema.prisma:943-958) — a many-to-many join model between Product and Supplier with per-supplier metadata:

```prisma
model SupplierProduct {
  id              String    @id
  supplierId      String
  productId       String
  supplierSku     String?
  lastUnitCost    Decimal
  currency        String
  leadTimeDays    Int?
  updatedAt       DateTime  @updatedAt

  @@unique([supplierId, productId])
}
```

This model is used by:
- Supplier-portal product editing (referenced in PR #154)
- Multi-supplier procurement (different lead times, costs, SKUs per supplier)
- Supplier-specific historical cost tracking

P8.2 proposes a SINGULAR `product.supplier` field updated by 'latest placed PO wins'. The spec doesn't reconcile:

1. **Is the new field a 'preferred/primary supplier' pointer that supplements SupplierProduct?**
   - If yes, semantics are: 'this is the most recent supplier we used; the SupplierProduct relation still lists every supplier we've ever bought from'
   - The reorder draft generator uses the new field; the SupplierProduct relation continues to store per-supplier metadata
   
2. **Is the new field replacing SupplierProduct?**
   - If yes, where does per-supplier metadata go (supplierSku, lastUnitCost, leadTimeDays)?
   - Multi-supplier procurement breaks: a product with 3 suppliers loses 2 of them
   - Supplier-portal features that depend on SupplierProduct break

3. **Migration story:**
   - Backfill product.supplier from: most recent PO line, OR the first SupplierProduct row, OR null?
   - If a product has SupplierProduct rows but no PO history yet (e.g., newly added to a supplier's catalog), what's the supplier field set to?

4. **Concurrent multi-supplier scenarios:**
   - A product genuinely sourced from 2 suppliers gets its 'supplier' field oscillating per-PO
   - Reorder forecasts generated per-supplier may exclude the product from the wrong supplier's draft

**Why this matters:**

The spec's brevity hides a significant data-modeling decision. Whether to ADD a field next to an existing relation or REPLACE the relation is the kind of choice that benefits from being explicit upfront — implementing the wrong interpretation costs a redo.

The cleanest interpretation given the existing schema: keep `SupplierProduct` for historical/per-supplier metadata; add `Product.preferredSupplierId` (or similar) as an explicit 'most-recent-PO supplier' pointer; the auto-update rule populates this pointer only.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update P8.2 in docs/plan.md to:
  - Explicitly state the relationship to SupplierProduct (supplement vs replace)
  - Specify the field name (`preferredSupplierId` or `latestPoSupplierId` may be clearer than `supplier`)
  - Document the backfill policy: derive from most recent PO line, with fallback to most-recently-updated SupplierProduct row
  - Address multi-supplier products: how do reorder drafts handle a product with multiple historical suppliers? Per-supplier slicing?
- Add transitions to P8.2's acceptance criteria for the SupplierProduct relation (does it stay, does it change?)
notes: Filed during PR #155 second-pass review (commit 65b726da). P8.2 is plan-only; this is about pinning the spec before implementation. Related to onetwo3d-ims-ljw (P8.1 migration path needed).

title: P8.1 product-lifecycle plan doesn't specify the migration path from existing NOT_FOR_SALE / ACTIVE / ARCHIVED enum (PR #155)
description:
PR #155 adds Group 21 / P8.1 to plan.md with a proposed new product-lifecycle status enum:

> Product statuses: `active`, `draft`, `eol`, `archived`

But the codebase already has `ProductLifecycleStatus { ACTIVE, NOT_FOR_SALE, ARCHIVED }` at `prisma/schema.prisma:36-40`, used by `Product.lifecycleStatus`. The plan item doesn't address:

1. **Mapping NOT_FOR_SALE → new states.** What happens to existing products in NOT_FOR_SALE? Some are arguably 'draft' (never published), some are arguably 'eol' (was published, removed from sale, still has stock). The plan needs a migration policy.

2. **`draft` overlap with existing publication flag.** The PR's description of draft says 'generated or being prepared, not published for sale yet, but can already be purchased.' This may overlap with:
   - WooCommerce's product status (publish/draft/private)
   - A separate IMS 'published' boolean if one exists
   - The existing NOT_FOR_SALE status

3. **Code paths assuming the current enum.** Search for `ProductLifecycleStatus`, `lifecycleStatus`, `ACTIVE`, `NOT_FOR_SALE`, `ARCHIVED` to find every existing consumer. Each needs updating to handle the new state machine. The plan acceptance criteria don't enumerate this surface.

4. **`isOperationalProductStatus` helper.** Referenced in app/actions/purchase-orders.ts — what's it currently doing, and how should it map to the new states?

5. **PO line creation / sales order creation / allocation** all probably check product status today. The plan doesn't enumerate which actions need updates.

**Why this matters:** P8.1's acceptance criteria say 'each allowed and blocked transition' — but the transition diagram is unstated. Without it, the implementer has to invent the mapping from existing data + existing code paths to the new state machine, which is exactly the kind of decision that benefits from upfront planning.

**Suggested additions to P8.1:**

1. **Migration table**:
   | Old | New |
   |-----|-----|
   | ACTIVE | active |
   | NOT_FOR_SALE | eol (default) — operators reclassify to draft if pre-publication |
   | ARCHIVED | archived |

2. **State transition diagram** explicitly listing which transitions are allowed:
   - draft → active (publish)
   - active → eol (start sell-off)
   - active → archived (immediate withdraw)
   - eol → archived (auto-archive job, or manual)
   - eol → active (un-EOL — undo)
   - archived → ? (allowed/disallowed?)

3. **Code-path inventory**: list every action/query that checks lifecycleStatus today and what it should do for each new state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update P8.1 in docs/plan.md with the migration table from current → new enum
- Add the explicit state transition diagram (allowed/disallowed transitions)
- Enumerate code paths that need updating (purchase-orders.ts, sales.ts, allocation.ts, product-list filters, etc.)
- Decide explicitly whether 'draft' is a new IMS concept or maps to an existing publication flag
notes: Filed during PR #155 review. The QG1 part of the PR is shippable; the P8.1 part is a plan-only addition that needs more spec before implementation.

title: invariant-preflight CI runs against a fresh empty DB — code-level gate only, not data-level (PR #155)
description:
PR #155's CI workflow runs:

```yaml
- run: npx prisma migrate deploy --schema prisma/schema.prisma
- run: npm run invariant-check:preflight
```

The DB is freshly migrated, has zero rows, and the invariant reporters scan an empty database. Every invariant check passes trivially because there's nothing to violate. The PR body acknowledges this: 'tenant data checks still need cron/operator runs against tenant databases'.

This is fine as a **code-level gate** (catches syntax errors, broken imports, queries that throw on empty data, new code paths that violate constraints at write time when the test suite exercises them). But it's NOT a data-level gate.

Concrete misnomers:

1. **Plan.md QG1 closure** says: `fail on report failures or critical invariant findings`. The PR body says the gate `blocks on critical findings/report failures`. Neither is wrong, but readers may assume the gate would catch a stock-quantity violation introduced by a refactor.

2. **An empty-DB invariant report will return 0 findings even if the refactor under PR breaks the invariant logic itself** — unless the refactor causes the reporter to throw, the gate stays green.

3. **The QG1 plan item** original intent: `Synthetic data with a known invariant violation; assert CI fails.` PR's tests assert CI fails ON A MOCKED RESULT but the actual CI workflow doesn't seed any violation.

**Why this matters:**

The gate provides a false sense of coverage for the kind of bug it's named after ('inventory invariant violation'). A reviewer seeing the green CI badge might assume the underlying data has been verified. In practice, the CI checks that the report **code** runs to completion against an empty DB.

**Suggested strengthening:**

- Seed a small fixture with known invariant violations (negative stock, orphan cost layer, refund status mismatch) → assert the preflight FAILS on this fixture
- Then RESET the fixture so the main preflight runs against an empty DB and passes
- Document explicitly in docs/development.md that the CI gate covers code correctness, not tenant data correctness
- Make the QG2 'per-phase regression test' convention the actual data-level safety net (tested with the focused unit tests on each PR)
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a CI step that seeds a known-violating fixture and asserts `invariant-check:preflight` exits non-zero
- Then reset/clear the fixture and run the real preflight (which should pass)
- Update docs/development.md to label the gate explicitly: 'code-correctness gate; data-level invariant checks live in scheduled cron jobs against tenant databases'
- Update plan.md QG1 entry to reflect the gate's scope
notes: Filed during PR #155 review. The gate has value (catches breaking code changes to invariant reporters) but the PR claims coverage broader than what it actually delivers.

title: Shopping invoice PDF nonce not tracked — replay within 5-minute TTL is unbounded (PR #154)
description:
PR #154's `parseShoppingInvoicePdfRequest` validates that the payload includes a `nonce` (non-empty string) but doesn't track which nonces have been seen.

```ts
// app/api/shopping/[connector]/invoice-pdf/route.ts
const parsed = parseShoppingInvoicePdfRequest(rawBody, connector, { now: dependencies.now })
if (!parsed.valid) return reject(dependencies, auditBase, parsed.reason)
// nonce is parsed but never checked against a 'seen' set
const order = await dependencies.findOrder(parsed.request)
```

The PR's stated security model includes the nonce, but the IMS side accepts any non-empty UUID-shape string. So a valid HMAC-signed payload can be REPLAYED:

- Same payload, same X-OTI-Signature, same timestamps
- IMS validates HMAC (✓ — body is unchanged)
- IMS parses payload (✓ — fields are still valid)
- IMS checks issuedAt+TTL (✓ — still within 5 minutes)
- IMS returns the PDF

So within the 5-minute TTL window, the SAME signed payload can be replayed N times. Rate limit caps at 60 req/min per IP, but:

- N = 60/min × 5min = up to 300 replays per IP for the same valid payload
- Across multiple IPs (proxied attacker, residential proxy networks), much higher

**Why this matters:**

For most flows this is moot — the PDF doesn't change, the customer downloads it once. But:

1. **PDF generation cost**: `loadInvoicePdf` reads from disk; cheap. If a future change generates the PDF on-demand, 300× replay = 300× generation cost.
2. **Activity log spam**: each verified-and-accepted attempt is logged. The audit trail bloats with duplicates.
3. **Combined with onetwo3d-ims-ecp (guest bypass)**: a compromised secret + enumerable guest order IDs means an attacker exfiltrates all guest invoices with bounded HMAC-signature work — and the nonce doesn't slow this down at all because they only need to sign once per order.

**Standard nonce-tracking:**

```ts
// On verify, check the nonce hasn't been seen in the TTL window
const existing = await db.shoppingInvoicePdfNonce.findUnique({ where: { nonce } })
if (existing) return reject(dependencies, auditBase, 'replay')
await db.shoppingInvoicePdfNonce.create({ data: { nonce, expiresAt } })
// Periodic sweep clears expired nonces
```

Or use a TTL-bounded cache (Redis SET NX with EX). Simple, cheap, defeats replay.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add nonce tracking to the shopping invoice PDF route:
  - Option A (db): new table 'shopping_invoice_pdf_nonces' with (nonce, expiresAt); index on nonce; periodic sweep
  - Option B (redis/in-memory): if rate-limit backend already uses redis, store nonces there with TTL = TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS + clock skew
- Tests: assert second request with same payload returns 'replay' rejection (or appropriate failure reason)
- Update the failure-reason union: add 'replay_detected'
- Document the nonce contract in lib/shopping-invoice-pdf.ts JSDoc
notes: Filed during PR #154 review. Mid-priority — replay is bounded by HMAC secret possession AND TTL AND rate limit. But the design intent of 'nonce' is one-shot, and the implementation is currently advisory only.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: shopping invoice PDF route consumes a one-shot nonce and rejects replay before storage access; verified by tests/security/shopping-invoice-pdf.test.ts.

title: Shopping invoice PDF HMAC reuses wc_webhook_secret — key reuse across two purposes (PR #154)
description:
PR #154's `lib/shopping-invoice-pdf.ts`:

```ts
const SHOPPING_INVOICE_SECRET_SETTING: Record<ShoppingConnectorId, string> = {
  woocommerce: 'wc_webhook_secret',
  shopify: 'shopify_webhook_secret',
}
```

The same `wc_webhook_secret` is used for:

1. **Incoming webhook verification**: WooCommerce → IMS posts an event, signs body with this secret, IMS verifies the signature.
2. **Outgoing PDF request signing**: WP plugin → IMS posts a PDF request, signs body with this secret, IMS verifies the signature.

This is **key reuse across distinct purposes** — a well-known cryptographic anti-pattern (RFC 4949 §3.4). Concrete risks:

1. **Cross-protocol replay**: a signed message intended for one purpose might be reinterpreted as another. WC webhook payloads and PDF request payloads are different JSON shapes, but:
   - A signed payload that happens to be valid JSON for BOTH schemas could be replayed across endpoints
   - The PDF schema is small (5-6 fields) — there's nontrivial overlap with hypothetical webhook payloads
   - Defense-in-depth principle: don't rely on schema differences to keep purposes separate

2. **Leak amplification**: if the webhook secret leaks (e.g., from a WordPress site backup, a wp-config dump, a compromised admin), the attacker gets BOTH:
   - Ability to forge webhooks (data injection)
   - Ability to forge PDF requests (data exfiltration — coupled with the guest-checkout bypass in onetwo3d-ims-ecp this is total invoice access)

3. **Rotation amplification**: rotating the webhook secret because of a webhook abuse incident also invalidates ALL pending PDF requests, breaking customer downloads. Same with rotating because of a PDF-request abuse incident. Coupling rotation events couples incident response.

**Cryptographic best practice:**

Each distinct cryptographic purpose gets its own key. Or use HKDF to derive purpose-specific subkeys from a single root:

```ts
hmacHex(`shopping-invoice-pdf:${connector}`, rootSecret)  // derive
// store derived key, use for signing/verifying PDF requests only
```

**Why this matters:** The HMAC contract here is the only authorization for the new shopping invoice PDF route. Cryptographic hygiene of the secret directly limits the abuse surface.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a NEW secret `wc_invoice_pdf_secret` (and `shopify_invoice_pdf_secret`) distinct from the webhook secret
- WP plugin's settings page should expose a separate field for this; OR derive the PDF secret from the webhook secret via HKDF with a purpose label
- Test that a payload signed with the webhook secret is REJECTED by the PDF route (cross-protocol replay defense)
- Document the key separation in docs/architecture.md and the helper plugin's README
- Migration: existing installations need a settings UI to set the new secret OR documentation for the derived-key approach
notes: Filed during PR #154 review. Pair with onetwo3d-ims-ecp because both undermine the PDF authorization layer, just from different angles.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: shopping invoice PDF HMAC uses wc_invoice_pdf_secret/shopify_invoice_pdf_secret rather than webhook secrets and rejects webhook-secret signatures; verified by tests/security/shopping-invoice-pdf.test.ts.

Add focused mocked coverage for at least one integration.

title: shouldGateWcEnable only checks first transition — config changes mid-stream bypass the gate (PR #152)
description:
PR #152's WC gate:

```ts
const WC_ENABLE_SETTING_KEYS = [
  'wc_sync_enabled',
  'wc_sync_product_enabled',
  'wc_stock_sync_enabled',
  'wc_fx_push_enabled',
] as const

async function shouldGateWcEnable(data: Partial<WcSyncSettings>): Promise<boolean> {
  const enabledKeys = WC_ENABLE_SETTING_KEYS.filter((key) => data[key] === 'true')
  if (enabledKeys.length === 0) return false
  const current = await getSettingValues([...enabledKeys])
  return enabledKeys.some((key) => current.get(key) !== 'true')
}
```

The gate fires only when AT LEAST ONE of the four enable-toggles TRANSITIONS from non-'true' to 'true'. Once a toggle is on, subsequent saves with the same toggle still 'true' DON'T fire the gate — even if other settings change.

**Concrete bypass:**

1. Operator tests WC connection with credentials X. Test passes.
2. Operator saves `wc_sync_enabled = true`. Gate fires (transition off→on), passes (fingerprint matches), settings saved.
3. Operator changes `wc_webhook_secret`, `wc_sync_interval_minutes`, or any other setting EXCEPT the 4 enable toggles. Calls `saveWcSyncSettings` again.
4. Gate does NOT fire (no toggle transition). Settings saved.

If the changed setting is `wc_webhook_secret` (which affects webhook payload validation), the operator has saved a new secret without verifying it works. WC starts sending webhooks; IMS rejects them because the secret doesn't match what WC was configured with.

**Why this matters:**

The gate's name suggests 'enabling sync requires a test'. But once enabled, any subsequent config change can land without retest. For settings that affect runtime behavior (webhook secret, base URL change, intervals), this is a real path to silent breakage.

**Related observation:** `saveWcCredentials` (separate action) DOES record its own test result. But settings changes via `saveWcSyncSettings` (different action) DON'T retrigger the gate.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decide policy:
  - **Option A (strict)**: gate fires on ANY save when ANY of the enable-toggles is currently true OR being set to true
  - **Option B (settings-specific)**: identify which settings affect connectivity/runtime and gate ONLY when those change AND something is enabled
  - **Option C (status quo)**: keep first-transition gating, but document the limit explicitly
- For SMTP/Mintsoft/Xero, audit the same pattern — when does the gate fire vs not?
- Add a regression test for each integration that exercises 'enabled-but-config-changed' → assert gate fires (per chosen policy)
notes: Filed during PR #152 review. The current shape catches the obvious 'enable for the first time' case but misses ongoing configuration drift.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: WooCommerce enable gate now runs when an enable setting is already true and config changes are saved; verified by tests/domain/integrations/woocommerce-connection-test-gate.test.ts.

title: Xero connection fingerprint doesn't include xero_client_secret — secret rotation bypasses the gate (PR #152)
description:
PR #152's Xero fingerprint:

```ts
async function buildXeroConnectionFingerprint(): Promise<string> {
  const [clientId, expectedTenantId, token] = await Promise.all([
    getSettingValue('xero_client_id'),
    getSettingValue('xero_expected_tenant_id'),
    db.accountingToken.findUnique({
      where: { connector: 'xero' },
      select: { tenantId: true, tenantName: true },
    }),
  ])
  return buildIntegrationConnectionFingerprint({
    clientId, expectedTenantId,
    tenantId: token?.tenantId ?? '',
    tenantName: token?.tenantName ?? '',
  })
}
```

The fingerprint includes `clientId`, `expectedTenantId`, `tenantId`, `tenantName` — but NOT `xero_client_secret`.

If an operator rotates `xero_client_secret` (e.g., after a suspected compromise, after a Xero app rotation) without re-testing, the fingerprint is UNCHANGED. The gate accepts the previous test's success result. The sync is enabled with an unverified secret.

If the new secret is correct, the sync just works (no harm). If the new secret is wrong (typo, copy-paste error), the sync FAILS on every API call but the gate said 'all good'. Operator sees confusing errors with no test-verified-baseline to compare against.

**Why this matters:**

The gate's stated purpose (per the PR description) is 'require successful current-fingerprint connection tests before enabling sync'. The intent is to prevent enabling with invalid settings. Missing `client_secret` from the fingerprint creates a path where settings change AND the gate accepts the old result.

Same audit needed for:
- **WooCommerce** (`buildWcConnectionFingerprint`): includes url/key/secret — but does the codebase have other WC settings that affect the connection? (e.g., wc_api_version, wc_user_agent, etc.) If yes, those should be in the fingerprint too.
- **Mintsoft** (`buildMintsoftConnectionFingerprint`): includes baseUrl/username/password/orderLookupConnector — but Mintsoft has additional config (webhook secrets, etc.) — check what's relevant.
- **SMTP**: includes host/port/user/pass/secure/fromName/fromEmail/replyTo — looks comprehensive.

**Concrete repro for Xero:**
1. Configure Xero with current valid client_id + client_secret. Test passes; status='success'; fingerprint stored.
2. Rotate `xero_client_secret` to a typo'd value via settings UI (assume no in-place validation).
3. Toggle xero_sync_enabled off → on. The gate checks fingerprint: unchanged (still the same client_id etc.). Status is success. Gate PASSES.
4. Next sync API call fails with 'unauthorized' from Xero.
acceptance_criteria:
- Audit each integration's fingerprint inputs against ALL settings that affect the connection:
  - Xero: add `clientSecret` (or its server-side HMAC per onetwo3d-ims-v6z)
  - WooCommerce: audit for missed settings
  - Mintsoft: audit for missed settings
- Document in lib/integration-connection-test-gate.ts JSDoc the contract for fingerprint inputs ('include every setting that affects connectivity')
- Add a focused test: for each integration, change each input field and assert the fingerprint changes
- Coordinate with onetwo3d-ims-v6z (secret-in-fingerprint) — the fix for that may change how secrets are included
notes: Filed during PR #152 review. Coupled with v6z because both concern the fingerprint's coverage of secret values.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: Xero fingerprint includes xero_client_secret via integrationConnectionFingerprintSecret, so secret rotation changes the gate fingerprint.

title: recordIntegrationConnectionTest does 4 separate upserts not in transaction — partial-write corrupts gate state (PR #152)
description:
PR #152's `recordIntegrationConnectionTest` writes 4 settings rows via `Promise.all` of 4 separate `upsert` calls:

```ts
await Promise.all(TEST_KEYS.map((suffix) =>
  repository.upsert({
    where: { key: testSettingKey(id, suffix) },
    create: { key: testSettingKey(id, suffix), value: values[suffix] },
    update: { value: values[suffix] },
  }),
))
```

Not wrapped in a Prisma `$transaction`. If 1-3 of the 4 upserts fail (e.g., connection blip mid-batch, lock timeout), partial state lands:

- `status: 'success'` written ✓
- `tested_at` written ✓
- `message` FAILED — keeps old value (e.g., 'Connection refused')
- `fingerprint` FAILED — keeps old value (or null on first test)

The subsequent `getIntegrationConnectionTestState` reads these as 4 independent values, returning a mixed state:

- `status: 'success'` + `fingerprint: <stale>` → gate compares against current expected fingerprint → MISMATCH → 'Retest the connection because settings changed' (false negative, user retests pointlessly)
- OR worse: `status: 'success'` + `fingerprint: null` if first-ever test → gate says 'Retest because the saved settings changed' — but the test DID pass; operator confused

The more dangerous case: previous test was `status: 'failed'` with the CURRENT fingerprint. Operator clicks Test, the upserts partially succeed — `status: 'success'` written, but message + fingerprint failed. Gate evaluates: status=success + fingerprint matches (the OLD fingerprint that happens to be the current one) → GATE PASSES even though the test result is half-recorded and incoherent.

**Why this matters:** The gate's job is to be a reliable trust signal. Partial writes that mix old + new fields produce reliability gaps in exactly the scenario the gate is meant to handle (settings flux).

**Concrete repro:**
1. Force a DB connection pool exhaustion during `recordIntegrationConnectionTest` (e.g., concurrent stress test)
2. Observe 4 settings rows where some have new value, others have old value
3. Hit any of the gate-checking save flows; observe the gate's verdict doesn't match what the operator just did
acceptance_criteria:
- Wrap the 4 upserts in `db.$transaction`:
  ```ts
  await db.$transaction(TEST_KEYS.map((suffix) =>
    db.setting.upsert({ ... })
  ))
  ```
  Or use Prisma's interactive transaction if the repository abstraction needs adjustments.
- Add a test that simulates upsert failure for one of the 4 keys and asserts that NONE land (atomic rollback)
- Consider: store the 4 fields as JSON in a single setting row instead of 4 rows. Single write, no atomicity concern, but loses the 'individual key' query convenience. Trade-off.
notes: Filed during PR #152 review. The Promise.all-of-upserts pattern is the same shape that's caused partial-state bugs in many systems. The fix is one transaction wrapper.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: connection-test setting writes are transaction-wrapped and rollback on partial failure; verified by tests/domain/integrations/connection-test-gate.test.ts.

Store last-test timestamp and result on settings records, add focused mocked tests, and tick the plan item when complete.

title: CSV export metadata tests assert via raw source-code regex — brittle, no behavior check (PR #151)
description:
PR #151's regression test for the route schemas (`tests/api/export-csv-metadata.test.ts:48-56`):

```ts
test('inventory report export route schemas do not repeat report metadata per CSV row', async () => {
  const stockPosition = await readFile('app/api/export/stock-position/route.ts', 'utf8')
  assert.doesNotMatch(stockPosition, /'totalValueBase', 'asOf', 'source', 'generatedAt', 'reservationQtySource'/)
  assert.doesNotMatch(stockPosition, /'movementCount', 'dateFrom', 'dateTo', 'generatedAt'/)
  // ... similar for inventory-ledger, inventory-costing
})
```

Three problems:

**1. The test reads source code as text, not behavior**
- A code reformat (single quotes → double quotes, line break inserted, schema array split across multiple lines) breaks the test even when behavior is correct
- A regression that drops the metadata HEADER while keeping the row schema cleanup PASSES — the negative assertion only checks that specific substring patterns aren't present in the source

**2. The negative-substring patterns are unstable**
- The regex `/'totalValueBase', 'asOf', 'source', 'generatedAt', 'reservationQtySource'/` requires EXACTLY this order with this whitespace. Any future schema reorder (e.g. moving 'reservationQtySource' earlier in the list) would make this pass even if 'asOf' is reintroduced
- The regex doesn't actually assert that the desired columns ARE present — only that an exact bad substring isn't

**3. No HTTP integration test**
The new behaviors (metadata header, row schema cleanup) deserve route-level tests:
- Call the route handler with a mock request
- Assert the response `Content-Type` is `text/csv`
- Assert the response body's first line matches the new column header list
- Assert the response header `X-IMS-Export-Metadata` contains the expected JSON
- Assert the metadata JSON does NOT contain row-level fields that should remain per-row

The first two tests (`csvResponse` / `csvBufferedStreamResponse` lower-level helpers) are correct — they test behavior. The route-level test is the gap.

**Why this matters:** Future contributors looking at the test signal will see 'green tests' and assume the route schema is locked. But the schema can drift arbitrarily as long as it doesn't match the exact substring patterns the test happened to encode.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replace the source-code regex test with a route-level integration test:
  - Mock the report builder to return a fixed report
  - Invoke the route's GET handler
  - Assert response body's first CSV line equals the expected schema string
  - Assert response header X-IMS-Export-Metadata equals the expected JSON
- Do this for at least one variant of each affected route (stock-position, inventory-ledger, inventory-costing)
- The original source-code regex test can stay as a smoke test if desired, but it should not be the only signal
notes: Filed during PR #151 review. Same 'test by inspection, not behavior' pattern as 9pk (PR #150). Filed independently because this one is a fresh source-code-string test that's strictly worse than a real integration test would be.
close_reason: Fixed: tests/api/export-csv-metadata.test.ts now calls stock-position, inventory-ledger, and inventory-costing export route wrappers with mocked report builders and asserts CSV headers plus decoded X-IMS-Export-Metadata behavior, rather than reading route source or constructing helper responses only.

title: Removal of metadata columns is a breaking CSV schema change without consumer migration guidance (PR #151)
description:
PR #151 removes columns that were previously in every export row:

- `stock-position`: drops `asOf`, `source`, `generatedAt` from stock-on-hand; `generatedAt` from stock-allocations; `dateFrom`, `dateTo`, `generatedAt` from negative-stock
- `inventory-ledger`: drops `openingQty`, `movementQty`, `closingQty`, `openingValueBase`, `movementValueBase`, `closingValueBase`, `generatedAt` from stock-movements; `generatedAt` from stock-adjustments, transfers, stock-counts
- `inventory-costing`: drops `asOf`, `source`, `valueReplayReliable`, `generatedAt` from inventory-valuation; `dateFrom`, `dateTo`, `groupBy`, `generatedAt` from cogs; `dateFrom`, `dateTo`, `generatedAt` from landed-cost

Anyone with existing consumer workflows that depend on these columns breaks:

1. **Excel / Power Query connections** — saved import templates with column mappings expect specific schemas; missing columns produce empty values or errors
2. **BI tools** (Looker, Metabase, Tableau, etc.) reading the CSV files have hardcoded column lists
3. **Custom scripts** (Python pandas, R, etc.) reading the exports for finance reporting
4. **Audit trails** — historical practice of looking at the CSV and seeing 'asOf' / 'generatedAt' embedded for every row no longer works; you have to inspect the HTTP header which most browsers' 'Save As' workflow discards

The metadata is now in the HTTP response header (`X-IMS-Export-Metadata`). But:
- Browsers' standard 'Save As CSV' file doesn't preserve HTTP headers
- BI tools' CSV importers don't read response headers
- A user downloading from the dashboard ends up with a CSV file that has lost the metadata entirely

**Why this matters:** P7.4's original framing was 'CSV exports embed report-level metadata per row' — the FIX is to remove the duplication. But the solution should preserve operator access to the metadata. Putting it in an HTTP header is correct for API consumers but loses information for the most common consumer (operator downloads a CSV).

**Possible mitigations:**
- Keep ONE header row of metadata at the top of the CSV (before the schema header row) — e.g. `# generatedAt=...; source=...; asOf=...`
- Or: include a sidecar JSON file in a multipart response (`Content-Type: multipart/mixed`)
- Or: write the metadata into the CSV filename (`stock-on-hand-2026-06-09T10-00-snapshot-source.csv`)

CHANGELOG entry is also missing for this breaking change to downstream consumers.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decide consumer-preservation strategy:
  - **Option A**: CSV preamble comment row(s) with metadata (sigil = '#') — most BI tools support skipping initial '#' lines
  - **Option B**: Filename encodes critical metadata (date, source) for at-a-glance file identification in the user's downloads folder
  - **Option C**: Accept the loss; add UI hint on the export button: 'Generated at: {timestamp}; source: {x}' so the operator can copy if needed
- Add a CHANGELOG entry calling out the schema change with guidance for affected consumers
- Update docs/architecture.md's CSV section to explain the operator-vs-API metadata access patterns
- Consider a backward-compat env var (`CSV_EXPORT_INCLUDE_METADATA_COLUMNS=true`) for tenants with rigid downstream consumers
notes: Filed during PR #151 review. The PR closes P7.4 by removing the duplication, but the solution favors API consumers over operator consumers. Worth getting finance/ops sign-off before this lands.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: CSV exports preserve operator-visible metadata in trailing # comment rows, expose API metadata through the encoded header, CHANGELOG.md documents the schema change and migration guidance, and docs/architecture.md documents operator-vs-API metadata access patterns.

title: X-IMS-Export-Metadata header has no size cap or encoding guard — risks broken responses (PR #151)
description:
PR #151 adds a custom HTTP response header carrying JSON-encoded report metadata:

```ts
// lib/csv.ts
if (metadata && Object.keys(metadata).length > 0) {
  headers[CSV_EXPORT_METADATA_HEADER] = JSON.stringify(metadata)
}
```

Used like:
```ts
csvBufferedStreamResponse(rows, schema, filename, {
  generatedAt: report.generatedAt,
  ...report.totals,
})
```

Two real risks:

**1. Size**
HTTP headers are typically capped around 8KB total (nginx `large_client_header_buffers`, AWS ALB ~16KB, etc.). The metadata is built from `{ ...report.totals }` — whatever the report defines. Today the totals are flat strings/numbers (e.g. `openingQty: '1.000000'`), well under 8KB. But:

- If a future change adds a structured field (e.g. `perWarehouseTotals: [{warehouseId: ..., qty: ...}, ...]` for a tenant with 100 warehouses), the header silently exceeds proxy limits
- nginx and most proxies reject the whole response with 502 / 500 when a single header exceeds the buffer
- The user sees a generic 'export failed' with no actionable error

**2. Encoding**
HTTP header values per RFC 7230 are limited to visible US-ASCII characters (or use proper RFC 8187 encoding). `JSON.stringify` of metadata can include:
- Non-ASCII warehouse / supplier names: `'café Berlin'`
- Currency symbols: `'£', '€', '¥'`
- Unicode in product names, customer names

Node.js's HTTP layer is lenient enough to send these as UTF-8 bytes, but strict reverse proxies (some CDNs, security appliances) will reject the response.

**Why this matters:** Today's headers fit; an analytics enhancement next quarter that 'just' adds a structured breakdown to totals will silently break exports for production tenants behind nginx/CDNs.

**Defensive approach:**
- Cap the JSON-encoded header to ~4KB; if exceeded, drop to a fallback (omit large fields, log a warning, or fall back to a small subset of essential metadata)
- Base64-encode the JSON payload to guarantee ASCII-safety, OR percent-encode it, OR use the RFC 8187 `filename*=UTF-8''...` style
- Document the contract in JSDoc: 'metadata must be a flat object with primitive values; total JSON encoding must be under N bytes'
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a size guard in `csvHeaders` that:
  - Computes the JSON-encoded length
  - If under threshold (e.g., 4KB), uses as-is
  - If over, either drops non-essential fields, truncates with an indicator, or omits the header and logs a warning
- Base64-encode the metadata value to guarantee header-safe characters
- Update tests in `tests/api/export-csv-metadata.test.ts` to:
  - Assert a large metadata payload is rejected/truncated gracefully
  - Assert non-ASCII content (e.g. `generatedBy: 'café Admin'`) round-trips safely
- Document the contract in CSV_EXPORT_METADATA_HEADER's JSDoc
notes: Filed during PR #151 review. Not blocking today's working examples but the contract has no upper bound, which is the foot-gun.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: lib/csv.ts base64url-encodes X-IMS-Export-Metadata, caps JSON metadata at 4096 bytes with truncation fallback, exposes metadata headers for browser clients, and tests cover oversized and non-ASCII metadata behavior.

- Or: have `emptyFinanceAnalyticsReportForSourceLimit` accept a 'sentinel value' string and zero-fill all expected totals keys derived from a TypeScript-generated keyof report['totals']

title: parseDateOnly silently accepts invalid dates like 2026-13-45 due to JS Date normalization (PR #150)
description:
PR #150 introduces `lib/domain/math/date-window.ts`:

```ts
const DATE_ONLY_RE = /^(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})$/

export function parseDateOnly(value, fallback, options = {}) {
  if (!value) return fallback
  const match = DATE_ONLY_RE.exec(value)
  if (!match) return fallback
  const parsed = new Date(Date.UTC(Number(match[1]), Number(match[2]) - 1, Number(match[3])))
  return options.endOfDay ? endOfUtcDay(parsed) : startOfUtcDay(parsed)
}
```

The regex accepts ANY 4-2-2 digit pattern. JS's `Date.UTC()` then *normalizes* out-of-range values silently:

- `Date.UTC(2026, 12, 45)` (i.e. input `2026-13-45`) → month 12 wraps to January of next year, day 45 wraps further → `2027-02-14`
- `Date.UTC(2026, 1, 30)` (i.e. `2026-02-30`) → `2026-03-02`
- `Date.UTC(2026, 0, 0)` (i.e. `2026-01-00`) → `2025-12-31`
- `Date.UTC(2026, 13, 32)` (i.e. `2026-14-32`) → `2027-03-04`

All silently accepted. The user typed something nonsensical; the system computes a real date months or years from what they meant; the report shows totally different data than expected.

**Why this matters:**

Finance analytics, VAT reports, and AR/AP aging are all driven by these date params via URL query strings or CSV export filters. A copy-paste error or typo in a date field doesn't fail; it returns a confidently-wrong period.

For an analytics report this isn't catastrophic (user sees odd data and can investigate), but for a VAT export filed with HMRC based on the wrong period, the consequences are real.
acceptance_criteria:
- After parsing, validate the result round-trips: `parsed.getUTCFullYear() === year && parsed.getUTCMonth() === month - 1 && parsed.getUTCDate() === day`. Reject if mismatch.
- Optionally: accept ISO date string with separators '-' OR validate via `Date.parse(value)` + cross-check
- Add tests for: `2026-13-45`, `2026-02-30`, `2026-00-15`, `2026-01-32` — all should fall back to the fallback value
- Same fix applies to `parseOptionalDateOnly`
notes: Filed during PR #150 review. The parsing pattern is duplicated from existing analytics modules (PR is consolidating them) — the bug pre-existed but is now in one place where it can be fixed at the source.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: parseDateOnly/parseOptionalDateOnly validate round-tripped UTC date components and reject normalized invalid dates; verified by tests/domain/math/date-window.test.ts.

title: Xero daily batch group A2 missing explicit orderBy — split-batch referenceId hash is non-deterministic (PR #149)
description:
PR #149's `lib/connectors/xero/daily-sync.ts` adds batching to all three daily batch groups. Each group's `referenceId` is derived from `buildDailyBatchReferenceId(group, date, entityIds)` where `entityIds` is the ordered list of IDs in the current window.

For determinism (same batch → same referenceId → idempotency works on retry), the input order MUST be stable across runs.

**Group A1** (line ~507) adds: `orderBy: [{ paidAt: 'asc' }, { id: 'asc' }]` ✓
**Group B** (line ~755) already had: `orderBy: { createdAt: 'asc' }` ✓
**Group A2** (line ~610) adds `take: batchLimit + 1` but **NO orderBy** ✗

Postgres returns rows in an undefined order when no orderBy is specified. The actual order depends on:
- Table physical layout (heap order)
- Vacuum / cluster operations
- Index used by the planner
- Parallel query workers

Concrete failure:
1. First run at 09:00: A2 query returns 1000 orders in order [O1, O5, O3, O7, ...] → hash 'abc12345' → referenceId `A2-2026-06-09-abc12345`.
2. Run fails partway / commit rollback. accountingSyncLog never gets the entry.
3. Next run at 09:05: same 1000 orders returned in order [O5, O1, O7, O3, ...] (heap changed slightly) → hash 'def67890' → referenceId `A2-2026-06-09-def67890`.
4. Idempotency check against accountingSyncLog with referenceId='A2-2026-06-09-abc12345' finds nothing → posts journal. But also posts a second journal under 'A2-2026-06-09-def67890' on a subsequent rerun if heap shifts again.

**Why this matters:** The whole point of deterministic referenceIds (per the PR's stated goal: 'deterministic split-batch journal references') is to make retries idempotent. Without a stable orderBy on A2, the same batch can post multiple times under different referenceIds.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add explicit orderBy to A2's findMany: `orderBy: [{ revenueDeferredDate: 'asc' }, { id: 'asc' }]` (or similar stable composite)
- Add a test that asserts buildDailyBatchReferenceId returns the same value for the same set of entity IDs in any order (sort IDs inside the function), OR pin that the input must be pre-sorted
- Consider: have buildDailyBatchReferenceId sort entityIds internally so callers can't accidentally introduce non-determinism — defense in depth
- Audit the docs/plan.md plan group 15 completion claim against this gap
notes: Filed during PR #149 review. The PR adds determinism to A1 and Group B but missed A2. Low likelihood of triggering today (postgres heaps are usually stable) but the failure mode is silent duplicate-journal posting in Xero.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: Xero A2 batch query uses stable revenueDeferredDate/id ordering and buildDailyBatchReferenceId sorts entity IDs defensively; verified by tests/xero-daily-batch.test.ts.

title: Cron rate limit backend defaults to in-memory — multi-instance deployments bypass the limit (PR #149)
description:
PR #149's `enforceCronRateLimit` calls `checkRateLimit` from `@/lib/rate-limit`. The backend is selected by `RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND` env var, defaulting to `'memory'` (`lib/security/rate-limit.ts:23`):

```ts
const value = (source.RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND ?? 'memory').trim().toLowerCase()
if (value === '' || value === 'memory') return 'memory'
```

In a single-instance Next.js deployment (current production setup per CLAUDE.md: 'Runs as next start on machine at 10.0.3.99'), the memory backend works — all cron requests hit the same process.

In a multi-instance / horizontally-scaled deployment:
- Replica A sees 1/12 used at 12:00
- Replica B sees 0/12 used at 12:00 (separate memory)
- A cron daemon's HTTP request can hit either replica
- Effective rate limit is N×max where N is replica count
- Documented quota is meaningless

The PR doesn't document this caveat in:
- `docs/installation.md` (deployment guide)
- `docs/architecture.md` (cron section)
- `.env.example` (no mention of RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND for cron)
- The new `lib/cron-rate-limit.ts` JSDoc

**Why this matters:** A team scaling IMS to multiple replicas (for HA or perf) would expect rate limits to still work. The silent multiplication would only be noticed if Xero/Mintsoft start rejecting due to upstream API limits, traced back to IMS sending N× the rate. Or when the system runs hot on outbox processing.

**Mitigation cost:** Already-implemented. Just set `RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND=redis` + `REDIS_URL=...` per `lib/security/rate-limit.ts:49`. Cost is documentation, not code.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a NOTE in lib/cron-rate-limit.ts JSDoc: 'Uses the application's rate-limit backend. For multi-replica deployments, set RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND=redis and provide REDIS_URL.'
- Update .env.example with RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND and REDIS_URL examples
- Update docs/installation.md cron section to document the multi-instance caveat
- Optional: add a preflight assertion (`scripts/preflight-production.ts`) that warns if multiple replica indicators (e.g., env hint like REPLICA_COUNT > 1) are set but RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND=memory
notes: Filed during PR #149 review. Not a code bug — a documentation gap. Single-instance prod (current state) is fine; this becomes a footgun the moment IMS scales out.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: cron rate-limit docs/JSDoc and .env.example document RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND=redis plus REDIS_URL for multi-replica deployments; verified by tests/security/rate-limit.test.ts and tests/security/cron-rate-limit.test.ts.

title: delivery-status cron quota of max=2 is half the expected 4/hr for a 15-min cadence (PR #149)
description:
PR #149 sets:

```ts
const rateLimitErr = await enforceCronRateLimit('delivery-status', { max: 2 })
```

(`app/api/cron/delivery-status/route.ts`)

But per docs/installation.md and the PR description ("schedule-compatible hourly quotas for 5-minute/15-minute cron jobs"), the delivery-status cron is scheduled every 15 minutes — that's 4 expected invocations per hour, not 2.

With max=2, the cron is silently denied half its scheduled runs. The denied invocations return 429, get logged as errors, but the actual delivery-status polling (which checks for shipment tracking updates from carriers) only completes twice per hour instead of four times. Tracking updates are delayed by 15-30 minutes more than the documented schedule.

**Why this matters:** Users tracking shipments via the IMS dashboard see stale tracking states. The cron appears to be working (some invocations succeed) but the cadence is silently halved. The rate-limit log entries explaining 429s are buried under normal noise.

This is a likely typo or copy-paste from the daily-cron max=1 default rather than a deliberate quota choice.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update `delivery-status` quota to max=5 (4 expected + 1 jitter headroom) or max=6 (1.5x cadence)
- Add a test or comment documenting the cadence-to-quota relationship
- Audit other per-route overrides to confirm they match their actual cron schedules:
  - mintsoft-* (likely 5-min or hourly per integration)
  - accounting-payment-poll (max=4 — likely 15-min cadence; check)
  - accounting-sync (max=12 — 5-min; addressed in dsd)
- Consider deriving max from a documented cadence per route rather than hard-coding numbers
notes: Filed during PR #149 review. Related to dsd. delivery-status is the clearest example of quota-cadence mismatch; the same audit should hit other per-route overrides.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: delivery-status uses CRON_RATE_LIMIT_FIFTEEN_MINUTE_MAX=6 for 15-minute cadence headroom; verified by app/api/cron/delivery-status/route.ts and tests/security/cron-rate-limit.test.ts.

title: Cron rate-limit quotas for 5-min crons have zero headroom for scheduling jitter (PR #149)
description:
PR #149 sets quotas matching exact schedule cadence:

- `mintsoft-webhook-sweeper`: max=12, window=60min → 5-minute cron, exactly 12 expected per hour
- `shopping-webhook-inbox`: max=12, same shape
- `accounting-sync`: max=12, same shape

The sliding-window rate-limiter (`checkRateLimit` backend) checks the last 60 minutes. If a cron runs at 12:00, 12:05, 12:10, ..., 12:55 (12 invocations), the 13th invocation at 13:00 still falls within the rolling 60-min window from 12:00 UNTIL that 12:00 entry expires — and "expires" is implementation-dependent (memory backend uses fixed timestamps; behavior at the boundary varies).

Concrete failure modes:

1. **Schedule jitter**: cron invocations rarely fire at exact 5-minute marks. A run at 12:00:00 + jitter (12:00:42, 12:05:18, etc.) accumulates 12 inside a 59-minute span; the legitimate 13:00 call hits the limit if any of the 12 previous ran late.
2. **Previous run took >5 min**: if the sweeper for one round takes 6 minutes (large queue, retries), the next cron at +5 finds the previous still mid-run OR the rate-limit window hasn't moved enough — denied.
3. **Cron daemon retries**: some cron daemons (e.g., systemd timers with OnFailure) retry transient failures. A failed-then-retried invocation consumes 2 budget units in <1 minute.

**Why this matters:** The rate-limit goal is preventing accidental over-invocation, but a 12/hr cap on a 12-per-hour cron defeats legitimate scheduled runs in the presence of normal operational variance. The 429 response with retry-after means the next legitimate run is also delayed.

The test (`tests/security/cron-rate-limit.test.ts`) doesn't exercise the boundary — it just checks the 429 shape, not the schedule-vs-quota interaction.
acceptance_criteria:
- Bump max for 5-minute crons to 14-15 (~20% headroom) — accounts for jitter without admitting double-frequency abuse
- Bump max for 15-minute crons (delivery-status at max=2) to 5-6 — current max=2 is well below the expected 4/hr
- Add a test scenario asserting 12 invocations within 5 seconds (simulating burst from jitter) still allow legitimate later runs
- Document the quota rationale in lib/cron-rate-limit.ts JSDoc so future contributors don't tighten to 'match cadence exactly'
notes: Filed during PR #149 review. The default 1/hr for daily/hourly crons is generous and safe; the per-route overrides for sub-hourly crons are the gap.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: five-minute cron quota uses CRON_RATE_LIMIT_FIVE_MINUTE_MAX=15 with documented jitter headroom; verified by tests/security/cron-rate-limit.test.ts.

title: Restore token IP-binding has 'unknown' fallback that collides between un-IPed requests (PR #148)
description:
PR #148 binds restore tokens to client IP via:

```ts
function restoreTokenClientIp(request?: Pick<NextRequest, 'headers'> | null): string {
  if (!request) return 'unknown'
  return getClientIp(request.headers) ?? 'unknown'
}
```

The literal string `'unknown'` is used as the IP when:
1. `request` is null/undefined (the GET handler signature `async function GET(req?: NextRequest)` makes req optional)
2. `getClientIp` returns null (proxy strips headers, request comes from internal IPC, etc.)

Then in token verification:
```ts
function restoreTokenPayloadMatches(actual, expected) {
  return ... && actual.clientIp === expected.clientIp
}
```

Two requests where `getClientIp` returns null both get `clientIp: 'unknown'`. They match each other. So:
- Admin A requests a restore token from a header-stripped path (binding: clientIp='unknown')
- Admin B initiates a restore on a header-stripped path (binding check: clientIp='unknown')
- Token issued for A is consumable by B

This is a niche scenario — both admins need to hit the un-IPed path. But the same admin can issue a token from one network and consume from another if both happen to lose headers. And tests can accidentally exercise this collision (test fixtures often skip request headers).

**Why this matters:** IP-binding is meant as defense-in-depth. The 'unknown' fallback makes it a no-op for any request path that doesn't extract the IP correctly. Better to fail-closed (reject token issuance / consumption when IP can't be extracted).
acceptance_criteria:
- Reject restore token issuance when client IP can't be extracted (return 400 'Cannot issue restore token without verifiable client IP')
- Reject restore token consumption with the same condition
- Update tests to provide explicit IPs in fixtures rather than relying on the 'unknown' fallback
- Same fix applies to PR #146's invoice PDF token IP binding (`getClientIp(...) ?? 'unknown'` is the same fallback there) — file companion issue or note in onetwo3d-ims-qv1
notes: Filed during PR #148 review. Lower priority than fpr/8vp because the threat requires the request path to lose IP headers AND multiple admins to hit that path. But the fix is a 5-line change to fail-closed.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: restore token issuance/verification fails closed without a verifiable client IP; verified by tests/api/backup-restore.test.ts.

title: Remote backup manifest upload is unguarded — orphan SQL on upload failure (PR #148)
description:
PR #148's `app/api/backup/upload-remote/route.ts` chains two upload calls:

```ts
const result = await uploadBackupToTarget(filePath, safe, target)
await uploadBackupToTarget(
  backupManifestPath(filePath),
  `${safe}.manifest.json`,
  target,
  'application/json',
)

await logActivity({ ... description: `Uploaded backup ... to ${target}` })
```

If the FIRST upload (SQL) succeeds but the SECOND upload (manifest) throws:
- The SQL backup is on the remote
- The manifest is NOT on the remote
- The error bubbles up to the operator
- But the SQL file is orphaned on the remote with no manifest sidecar

Same pattern in `app/api/cron/backup/route.ts` cron path — if SQL upload succeeds and manifest upload fails, cron returns the SQL upload error and the orphan stays.

**Why this matters:** Future restore from the remote (via download → upload to restore endpoint) requires the manifest. An orphan SQL on the remote can't be restored cleanly via the stored-backup path because there's no manifest to validate.

The orphan also doesn't trigger any cleanup or alert — operator has to manually notice and either re-upload or delete.
acceptance_criteria:
- Wrap the two-upload sequence in a try/catch
- If the manifest upload fails after SQL upload succeeded, delete the orphan SQL file from the remote (or queue a retry of the manifest upload)
- Log the failure with sufficient detail to manually recover (`level: 'ERROR'` with metadata: { target, sqlKey, manifestKey })
- Consider: a single `uploadBackupArtifactsToTarget()` helper that takes both files and either uploads both or neither
- Add a test that asserts cleanup happens on manifest upload failure
notes: Filed during PR #148 review. Same anti-pattern as PR #128/#129/#142's silent activity-log catches and PR #137's enqueueStockSync outside transaction — partial-success operations need explicit recovery.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: remote backup artifact upload cleans up the SQL artifact if manifest upload fails; verified by tests/backup-remote.test.ts.

title: Disk-space preflight uses file.size * 2 multiplier — wrong for SQL restores (PR #148)
acceptance_criteria:
- Replace the file.size * 2 heuristic with an estimate based on backup-time database size (stored in manifest? add `databaseSizeBytes` field?)
- For initial implementation, use a conservative multiplier: file.size * 10 catches more realistic cases
- Document the heuristic limit explicitly in the preflight error message ("requires ~Nx the SQL file size in free disk")
- Consider a separate `databaseSizeBytes` field in the manifest that the preflight reads directly
notes: Filed during PR #148 review. The check is well-intentioned but its multiplier doesn't match how SQL restores consume disk. A failed restore mid-way is worse than a rejected restore upfront.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: restore disk preflight uses manifest databaseSizeBytes plus conservative SQL-size multiplier instead of file.size*2; verified by tests/api/backup-restore.test.ts.

title: Backup manifest row counts collected separately from pg_dump — snapshot drift between dump and manifest (PR #148)
description:
PR #148's flow in `app/api/backup/create/route.ts` and `app/api/cron/backup/route.ts`:

1. `execFile('pg_dump', args, ...)` — pg_dump runs in its own transaction snapshot.
2. After pg_dump finishes successfully, `writeBackupManifestForFile(filePath, filename, prisma)` runs.
3. The manifest function executes `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <table>` for every table.

The pg_dump snapshot and the manifest's COUNT queries are TWO DIFFERENT transaction snapshots. Between pg_dump finishing and the manifest collecting row counts (could be seconds for a large DB), writes can land that mismatch the manifest from the actual dump contents.

Concrete scenarios:
- A backup runs at 02:00. pg_dump dumps `sales_orders` with N rows. A new order lands at 02:01. The manifest writes `sales_orders.rowCount = N+1`. A restore that checks "actual rows match manifest rowCount" would fail or pass spuriously depending on which side is right.
- Currently no restore-side validation actually compares the manifest's rowCount against post-restore counts — it just checks the manifest exists and critical tables are listed. So the snapshot mismatch is silent today. But the manifest claims to represent the dump.

**Why this matters:** Future enhancements ("restore validates row count parity") would build on a foundation that doesn't actually guarantee snapshot consistency. The audit story of the manifest is also weaker — it's a snapshot of the database TAKEN AFTER pg_dump finished, not OF the dump itself.
acceptance_criteria:
- Use pg_dump's `--snapshot=<id>` flag to export with a known snapshot, then run the manifest queries against the same snapshot via `SET TRANSACTION SNAPSHOT '<id>'` in a Prisma transaction
- Alternatively, run pg_dump and the manifest queries inside a single shared read-only transaction with REPEATABLE READ isolation
- Add a test that exercises the race: write rows between pg_dump completion and manifest write, assert the manifest matches the dump (or document that it doesn't and the rowCount is advisory)
- Document the consistency model in BackupManifest type's JSDoc
notes: Filed during PR #148 review. Operational impact is low today (no restore-side validation uses rowCount). But this is foundational — fixing it later requires rebuilding manifest collection.
close_reason: Already resolved on development by documenting the manifest row-count consistency model as post-dump-advisory; restore validation relies on schema version, filename, critical tables, and databaseSizeBytes, verified by tests/backup-manifest.test.ts.

title: Implement plan group 14 backup restore hardening
description: Implement docs/plan.md group 14: P6.3 restore token TTL/session binding, P6.7 restore upload size and disk-space guard, and P7.7 backup manifest generation/restore validation. Update plan checkboxes, tests, Beads export, commit, push, and open PR against development.
close_reason: Implemented backup restore hardening group 14 with restore token binding, upload limits/disk checks, backup manifests, tests, and plan updates.

title: Add product end-of-life feature to plan
description: Add a planned feature to docs/plan.md for an End of Life product flag: product page checkbox, reorder forecast exclusion, filtering, and automatic archival once all warehouse stock is zero and no incoming stock remains.
close_reason: Added P8.1 to docs/plan.md for product End of Life checkbox, reorder forecast exclusion, EOL filtering, and automatic archival once stock and incoming supply are exhausted.

title: Password policy common-password list contains 7 entries — order-of-magnitude too small (PR #146)
description:
PR #146 adds `lib/security/password-policy.ts` with:

```ts
const COMMON_PASSWORDS = new Set([
  'password',
  'password1',
  'password12',
  'password123',
  'password123!',
  'adminadmin123!',
  'onetwoinventory1!',
])
```

7 entries. The intent is the right one (block obviously-bad passwords) but the list is way too small to materially improve security:

- The well-known SecLists 'common-passwords-top-10000' has 10k entries — none of which (except the seed entries above) are caught.
- Real attackers use lists with 100k–1M entries.
- The RockYou leak alone contains 14M entries.

Effect: `'Letmein123!'`, `'Qwerty123!'`, `'P@ssword123'`, `'Welcome2024!'` — none caught by this list. Each meets all the structural requirements (length, uppercase, number, symbol) and passes validation.

**Why this matters:** The plan item P6.6 specifically calls for stronger password requirements. A 7-entry common-password list satisfies the letter (some check exists) but not the spirit (real common passwords are blocked) of the requirement.
acceptance_criteria:
- Either:
  - **Option A** (preferred): integrate HaveIBeenPwned Pwned Passwords k-anonymity API — submit first 5 chars of SHA1 hash, get list of suffixes, check locally. Catches actual breached passwords without sending the full password anywhere.
  - **Option B**: bundle a static list of top 10k common passwords (e.g., from SecLists). Lower fidelity than HIBP but better than 7.
  - **Option C**: keep the 7-entry list AS A FLOOR plus dictionary checks (no plain English words, max repeated characters, etc.)
- Add tests asserting the policy rejects common attack-list passwords (e.g., 'Welcome2024!', 'Qwerty123!', 'P@ssword123')
- Also add (probably worth a separate issue): max password length (e.g., 256) to prevent bcrypt DoS via long inputs
notes: Filed during PR #146 review. The implementation works as written, but the protection is illusory for real attacker password lists.
close_reason: Already fixed on development: password policy includes max length, broader common-password/pattern checks, and tests for Welcome/Qwerty/P@ssword variants; verified by tests/security/password-policy.test.ts.

title: Invoice PDF token max TTL hard-capped at 10 minutes with no admin override (PR #146)
description:
PR #146 sets:

```ts
const DEFAULT_INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 10 * 60
const MAX_INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 10 * 60
```

Both default and max are 10 minutes. The old values were 3-day default and 30-day max. The 30-day max was clearly too lax, but 10-min max means:

1. **No admin override possible** — if a tenant needs longer TTL for a specific workflow (e.g., finance team scheduled an invoice review for tomorrow), there's no env or settings configuration that allows it.
2. **Email-an-invoice-link workflows break** — operator emails themselves an invoice link from desktop, opens it on phone 15 minutes later → expired.
3. **Batch download flows break** — opening multiple invoices, leaving tabs in background while doing other work, then downloading → first tabs expired.

Combined with the IP-binding fragility (`onetwo3d-ims-qv1`), the link is effectively single-use within ~5 minutes from same device, same network. That's appropriate for the highest-security path but not configurable for tenants with more relaxed threat models.
acceptance_criteria:
- Make max TTL configurable via env var (e.g., `INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_MAX_TTL_SECONDS`) with a sensible production default
- Document the env var in `.env.example` and `docs/installation.md`
- Keep the 10-minute DEFAULT (most operators won't override) but allow tenants to raise the cap when needed
- Consider: separate TTL for 'fresh download from invoice page' (short, 10min) vs 'recovery from emailed link' (longer, 1h-24h)
notes: Filed during PR #146 review. The PR's stated rationale ('shortens max/default TTL to 10 minutes') is sensible for the default; the lack of any override path is the operational risk.
close_reason: Fixed: invoice PDF token maximum TTL is configurable via INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_MAX_TTL_SECONDS with a 30-day hard cap while preserving the 10-minute code default; docs and .env.example updated; verified by tests/security/invoice-pdf-token.test.ts.

title: Invoice PDF token IP-binding is operationally fragile for legitimate users (PR #146)
description:
PR #146 binds invoice PDF tokens to both the session and the client IP. The IP binding breaks token validity for legitimate users in several realistic scenarios:

1. **Mobile users**: a user opens the invoice list on home WiFi, then their phone switches to cellular (different IP), then they tap the invoice link → token rejected with `wrong_ip`.
2. **Corporate NAT / load balancers**: enterprise networks rotate the apparent client IP on subsequent requests through different egress routes → token rejected mid-session.
3. **VPN reconnect**: VPN clients drop and reconnect with a new IP regularly → broken links.
4. **Roaming / hotspot handoff**: mobile networks shift IPs every few minutes on long-running pages.
5. **Tab restored after device sleep**: IP may have changed.

The threat model the binding addresses is 'shared link from a stolen URL'. But session-binding alone already prevents this for non-authenticated attackers (they don't have the session cookie). For authenticated attackers who do have the session, IP binding helps marginally but at significant operational cost.

**Why this matters:** The PR description says PDF links 'are no longer generated into WooCommerce customer notes because the hardened token now requires current session/IP binding'. That's an explicit acknowledgment that the binding is too strict for non-interactive use. But the same strictness applies to interactive users on mobile/VPN/corporate networks.

**Refs:**
- `lib/invoice-pdf.ts` — `bindingHash('clientIp', ...)` in signing and verification
- `app/api/invoices/[id]/route.ts` — `getInvoicePdfTokenBinding` extracts clientIp from `getClientIp(request.headers)`
acceptance_criteria:
- Decide on the threat model explicitly: is IP binding worth the operational cost given session binding is already enforced?
- If yes (keep IP binding): document the operational caveats in user-facing help docs ('if you switch networks, refresh the invoice list to get a new link')
- If no (drop IP binding): keep session binding; remove the `ipHash` field and `wrong_ip` reason
- Consider middle ground: bind to /24 subnet rather than exact IP — catches most legitimate IP changes (mobile carrier subnet stays similar), still blocks attackers from another network
- Add a 'PDF link expired or invalid — please request a new one from the invoice page' user-facing error that operators see when the binding fails (currently the route returns 404 / 403 which is opaque)
notes: Filed during PR #146 review. The same trade-off was implicitly acknowledged by the PR's own removal of WC customer-note links. Worth making the trade-off explicit rather than discovering it via support tickets.
close_reason: Fixed: kept session+client-IP binding as the chosen IMS admin threat model, documented the network-change/VPN caveat and shopping handoff alternative, and changed rejected IMS invoice PDF links to return a user-facing refresh/request-new-link error; verified by tests/security/invoice-pdf-token.test.ts.

title: Implement production plan group 13 security hardening
description: Why this issue exists: docs/plan.md group 13 is the next unimplemented production-readiness batch after groups 1-12. It covers P6.2 supplier portal tenant boundaries, P6.4 invoice PDF token leakage mitigation, P6.5 generic WooCommerce connector errors, P6.6 stronger password requirements, and P6.8 activity-log metadata redaction. What needs to be done: implement the security hardening batch with focused tests, mark the covered plan items/group complete, run validation, commit, push, and open a PR against development.
close_reason: Implemented production plan group 13 security hardening: supplier boundary assertions, session/IP-bound invoice PDF tokens, generic WooCommerce config errors, stronger password policy, and activity-log secret redaction coverage.

title: No daily cron creates account balance snapshots — tightened freshness window will fail in production (PR #145)
description:
Codex adversarial review of PR #145 (P5.3) identified:

The PR tightens the snapshot freshness window from 7 days to 1 day (previous-day required). But snapshot creation depends on a manual UI sync button (`app/(dashboard)/sync/xero-client.tsx:352`) and `app/actions/xero-sync.ts` — no scheduled cron handler exists in `app/api/cron/` for account balance snapshots specifically.

Consequence: tenants that don't run a manual sync every day — or any fresh-install tenant — will have no previous-day snapshot. With this PR merged, every GL period-movement query will either return null (current behaviour) or fail loudly (if bd-304 is addressed).

This is a deployment dependency gap: the code tightens the contract without the operational mechanism to satisfy the contract.

**Why this matters:** A tenant that never manually clicks Sync will see their COGS variance / GL period reports break silently (current) or loudly (after bd-304). Either way, the report is unavailable until someone clicks Sync — which they may not realise is required.
