- Zero VAT charged to the customer (the IMS taxBase already reflects this when the TaxRate.rate is 0)
- The invoice still appears on the VAT return in box 6 and box 8 for the customer to self-account

Fix direction:
1. lib/connectors/xero/invoices.ts:prepareSalesInvoicePayload — when the source line's resolved TaxRate has reverseCharge=true, prefer the per-component accountingTaxType over the parent's; if a component is missing, fall back to a configured reverse-charge tax type setting.
2. Same change in lib/connectors/xero/bills.ts:prepareXeroBillPayload for purchase-side reverse charge (EU acquisitions, services from outside UK).
3. Settings: add accounting_xero_reverse_charge_sales_tax_type and accounting_xero_reverse_charge_purchase_tax_type so admins can configure the chart-of-accounts mapping.
4. Activity log: include reverseCharge=true on the sync metadata so the rejected-sync dashboard (PR #177) and reconciliation reports can identify these.

This is sub-task B of the original onetwo3d-ims-w3p umbrella. Sub-task A (TaxComponent breakdown) is x8m. Sub-task C (VAT report categories) is delivered by the PR following x8n.
acceptance_criteria: Reverse-charge sales/purchase invoices push to Xero with the configured reverse-charge tax type on each line; the VAT report (after w3p-C lands) shows them under the REVERSE_CHARGE reporting category; a test asserts the payload tax type swap when the parent TaxRate has reverseCharge=true.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #183: sales/purchase invoice payloads swap taxType to settings.reverseCharge{Sales,Purchase}TaxType when resolved TaxRate.reverseCharge is true; defensive fallback to original accountingTaxType when setting is empty.

title: Wire downstream consumers of tax rate component profiles (connector + reporting)
description:
PR #180 closed onetwo3d-ims-4nf by adding the TaxRateComponent schema, resolver plumbing, settings UI, and effective-rate compound math. However, no downstream code actually reads the new profile fields:

1. Xero / QuickBooks connector payloads still send the parent TaxRate.accountingTaxType only. For compound profiles (e.g. GST 5% + PST 7% compound = 12.35%), the accounting payload sends a single tax code rather than splitting into Xero's TaxComponent breakdown. Verified by grep: no connector code reads .components, .reverseCharge, or component-level .accountingTaxType.

2. TaxRate.reverseCharge is stored on the model but no code reads it. UK-EU B2B reverse-charge invoices need to be posted to Xero with explicit reverse-charge tax codes (zero VAT charged + box 1 / box 8 reportable on the VAT return). Without consumption, the flag is a documentation field only.

3. TaxRate.reportingCategory (DOMESTIC / REVERSE_CHARGE / EC_SALES / OSS) is stored but no report groups or filters by it. The VAT report at lib/domain/finance/finance-period-analytics.ts:getVatReport sums by taxRatePercent — it cannot distinguish EC sales from domestic for OSS reporting.

Three concrete sub-tasks for follow-up implementations:

A. Connector: when an invoice's tax rate has active components, the Xero/QBO payload should produce one accounting line per component (each with its own accountingTaxType) OR emit a single TaxComponent payload Xero understands. Document the chosen approach; align with Xero TaxComponent and QB ItemBased tax model.

B. Reverse charge: when TaxRate.reverseCharge=true, the invoice payload to Xero should use the configured reverse-charge tax codes (settings), the IMS-side calculation should show zero VAT collected, and activity log should mark the line so VAT reports can include it in box 1/8.

C. Reporting: VAT report should accept a reportingCategory filter and group output by category. Add reportingCategory column to the report select / aggregation.

Each sub-task can ship as its own PR. This issue tracks the umbrella ambit of 4nf's original 'fix direction'.
acceptance_criteria: A. Xero invoice/bill payload generation reads tax rate components and produces appropriate line breakdown or TaxComponent metadata. B. reverseCharge flag is read by the accounting payload and tax math (zero VAT) and emits a marker the VAT report can pick up. C. getVatReport groups by reportingCategory and supports filtering; the analytics page shows the grouping.
close_reason: Fixed: full umbrella delivered across PRs #181 (C: VAT report categories), #182 (C UI), #183 (B: reverse charge), #184 (A: documented constraint + warning). Residual Xero TaxRate sync tracked as t6r.

title: Add purchase invoice edit UI and accounting update queue
description: IMS currently has purchase invoice creation and payment flows, but no purchase-invoice edit UI/action path. Xero PURCHASE_INVOICE_UPDATE processor support exists, but there is no reachable app path that lets an operator edit a purchase invoice/bill and queue an external bill update when accountingInvoiceId is already set. Add the UI and server action path with the same accounting/update semantics as sales invoice edits.
acceptance_criteria: Operators can edit allowed purchase invoice fields from IMS. Edits validate purchase workflow invariants, update the PurchaseInvoice and PurchaseInvoiceLine rows transactionally, and queue PURCHASE_INVOICE_UPDATE with a content-derived idempotency key when accountingInvoiceId exists and Xero purchase invoice sync is enabled. Tests cover editable fields, immutable/paid guardrails, accounting update queue creation, and no duplicate queue rows for unchanged content.
close_reason: fixed

title: Add orchestration test for updateInvoice (purchase bill edit) decision tree
description:
PR #178 added updateInvoice in app/actions/purchase-orders.ts to let users edit unpaid purchase bills. The three helpers in lib/domain/purchasing/purchase-invoice-edit.ts (assertPurchaseInvoiceEditable, hasPurchaseInvoiceEditChanges, buildPurchaseInvoiceUpdateIdempotencyKey) have unit tests, but the action's full decision tree is not exercised by any test.

The untested branches:
1. Xero connector + PURCHASE_INVOICE_UPDATE enabled → queueAccountingSyncTx called with type PURCHASE_INVOICE_UPDATE, expected idempotencyKey, and accountingInvoiceId-bearing payload.
2. QuickBooks (or no) connector → tx.activityLog.create with action purchase_invoice_update_skipped_unsupported_connector, no queueAccountingSyncTx call.
3. Xero connector + sync type disabled → no queueAccountingSyncTx, no warning log (silent skip matches sales-side pattern).
4. hasPurchaseInvoiceEditChanges = false → returns { success: true, notice: 'No bill changes to save' } without opening a transaction.
5. Overbilling guard → existing other-invoice quantities + new edit > poLine.qty → throws and rolls back.
6. assertPurchaseInvoiceEditable on the in-tx re-check rejects if paidAt was set between early validation and lock acquisition.

A regression that removes the overbilling check, flips the connector branch, or drops the queue call would not fail any existing test. Same shape as h7w (now closed) for the sales-side updateInvoice orchestration.
acceptance_criteria: An action-level test exercises the six branches above, mocking db.purchaseInvoice.findUnique, db.$transaction, the relevant tx delegates, getActiveAccountingConnectorInfo, isAccountingSyncTypeEnabled, queueAccountingSyncTx, tx.activityLog.create, and logActivity. A regression that removes the queueAccountingSyncTx call, drops the QBO warning log, or skips the overbilling guard fails at least one of these tests.
close_reason: Fixed: extracted purchase invoice update sync queue decision into an injectable helper and added tests for Xero queueing, unsupported connector warning logs, disabled sync-type no-op, and no-external-id skip. Overbilling and editability branches are covered by the shared purchase-invoice edit helper tests.

title: Add orchestration test for queueSalesInvoiceForOrder UPDATE branch
description:
PR #176 added the UPDATE branch in app/actions/sales.ts:queueSalesInvoiceForOrder — when so.accountingInvoiceId exists, build the document payload, derive a payload-hash idempotency key, check the active connector, gate on isAccountingSyncTypeEnabled('SALES_INVOICE_UPDATE'), then queueXeroSync({type: 'SALES_INVOICE_UPDATE', payload, idempotencyKey}) and log INFO sales_invoice_update_queued. Non-xero connectors log WARNING sales_invoice_update_skipped_unsupported_connector and return without queueing.

The building blocks (buildXeroIdempotencyKey, accountingPayloadKey) have unit tests but the action-level orchestration does not. A regression that removes the queueXeroSync call, flips the connector check, or drops the WARNING log would not fail any current test.

Add a unit test that mocks db.salesOrder.findUnique, getAccountingSettings, getActiveAccountingConnectorInfo, isAccountingSyncTypeEnabled, queueXeroSync, and logActivity. Assert: (a) Xero connector + enabled sync type → queueXeroSync called once with type SALES_INVOICE_UPDATE, the expected idempotencyKey prefix, and accountingInvoiceId folded into payload; logActivity called with sales_invoice_update_queued. (b) QuickBooks connector → no queueXeroSync; logActivity called with sales_invoice_update_skipped_unsupported_connector. (c) Xero connector + disabled sync type → no queueXeroSync; no error log (silent skip matches existing pattern).
acceptance_criteria: Tests exercise the three branches end-to-end at the action layer, asserting queueXeroSync call counts/args and logActivity actions. A regression that drops the queueXeroSync call or flips the connector check fails at least one of these tests.
close_reason: fixed

title: rebasePurchaseOrderStoredBaseAmounts must run inside a transaction with the parent update
description:
PR #173 added rebasePurchaseOrderStoredBaseAmounts in lib/domain/purchasing/purchase-order-fx-rebase.ts and wired it into updatePurchaseOrder (app/actions/purchase-orders.ts:1198). The helper writes line and freight cost base values immediately via db.purchaseOrderLine.update / db.freightCostLine.update, then returns the parent base totals so the caller can fold them into a deferred db.purchaseOrder.update at the bottom of updatePurchaseOrder.

Because the helper runs against the top-level db and the caller is NOT inside db.$transaction, the line/freight updates commit immediately while the parent base totals are still pending. Anything between those two points — tax-rate batch resolution, line VAT recompute, validation, optimistic concurrency error — that throws leaves the PO with line bases rebased to the new fxRateToBase but the parent (subtotalBase, taxBase, totalBase, directFreightBase, fxRateToBase) still at the OLD rate. The PO ends up internally inconsistent.

There is also no SELECT … FOR UPDATE on the PO during the rebase, so a concurrent updatePurchaseOrder can race (low concern given DRAFT-only edits but still worth noting).

Two fixes are equivalent:
1. Wrap the updatePurchaseOrder body in db.$transaction(async (tx) => { … }) and pass tx into rebasePurchaseOrderStoredBaseAmounts so all writes commit/rollback atomically.
2. Have rebasePurchaseOrderStoredBaseAmounts write the parent atomically alongside lines (move the db.purchaseOrder.update(parent base fields + fxRateToBase) into the helper inside its own transaction) and let the rest of updatePurchaseOrder carry only the non-rebase updates.

Option 1 is preferred — updatePurchaseOrder already does many sequential writes that ought to be in a single transaction, and tying the rebase to a tx aligns with how lockMutable{Sales,Purchase}Orders are used in other domains.
acceptance_criteria: Currency/rate-only PO edits commit line, freight, and parent base updates atomically. A test that injects a throw after rebasePurchaseOrderStoredBaseAmounts returns but before the final db.purchaseOrder.update demonstrates the rollback leaves all base values at the pre-edit fxRateToBase.
close_reason: fixed

title: Decouple invoice PDF security dashboard DB filter from emission invariant
description:
PR #172's invoicePdfTokenSecurityEventWhere() in app/actions/activity-log.ts narrows getRecentInvoicePdfTokenSecurityEvents to rows whose metadata.reason is one of INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_SECURITY_REASONS = ['wrong_session', 'wrong_ip']. The OLD post-filter accepted ANY invoice_pdf_token_security_signal action row regardless of reason; only invoice_pdf_token_rejected rows were reason-scoped.

Today this is a no-op because app/api/invoices/[id]/route.ts:isInvoicePdfTokenSecuritySignal() returns true only when verification.reason is wrong_session or wrong_ip, so every emitted security_signal event already has metadata.reason set to one of those two values. The dashboard now silently depends on isInvoicePdfTokenSecuritySignal() classifying the same reason set as INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_SECURITY_REASONS. If a future contributor adds a new security reason (e.g. wrong_subnet) to the signal classifier, those new signal events would be dropped from the dashboard with no test failure.

Two fixes are equivalent:
1. Restructure the DB predicate as OR-of-action-conditions: OR: [{ action: 'invoice_pdf_token_security_signal' }, { action: 'invoice_pdf_token_rejected', metadata: { path: ['reason'], in: [...] } }] so it mirrors the post-filter exactly.
2. Centralise the security reason list — have isInvoicePdfTokenSecuritySignal() import INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_SECURITY_REASONS, and add a test that asserts the two share a single source of truth.

Pick whichever fits the existing layering. Option 2 is closer to a single source of truth; option 1 preserves the old catch-all semantics.
acceptance_criteria: Adding a new reason to isInvoicePdfTokenSecuritySignal() either (a) automatically extends the dashboard filter via a shared constant, or (b) triggers a test failure that fails loudly if the two drift. Either way, future reason additions cannot silently drop dashboard visibility.
close_reason: Fixed: invoice PDF token security dashboard query now includes all security_signal rows and reason-filters only rejected rows, preventing future signal reasons from being silently hidden.

title: Tighten getRecentInvoicePdfTokenSecurityEvents SQL filter to metadata.reason
description:
PR #168's getRecentInvoicePdfTokenSecurityEvents pulls 100 rows where action is in ['invoice_pdf_token_security_signal', 'invoice_pdf_token_rejected']. The post-fetch summarizer (summarizeInvoicePdfTokenSecurityEvents) then drops any row whose metadata.reason is not 'wrong_session' or 'wrong_ip'. For a tenant with many ordinary expired/bad_signature rejections, the 100-row cap fills with rows the summarizer immediately filters out, so the surfaced summary can become incomplete even when the underlying security signals exist further back.

Add a SQL-level filter so the fetch only loads rows that the summarizer can actually use:

  metadata: { path: ['reason'], in: ['wrong_session', 'wrong_ip'] }

Not urgent — the cap means cost is bounded — but the surfacing fidelity grows with audit volume. Also adjust the take: 100 once the fetch is filtered (a smaller take may be sufficient with the tighter where clause).
acceptance_criteria: getRecentInvoicePdfTokenSecurityEvents adds a Prisma JSON path filter on metadata.reason so only relevant rows are fetched. A test asserts that with ordinary bad_signature/expired rejections present, none of them appear in the source rows the summarizer sees.
close_reason: Fixed: invoice PDF token security event query now filters at the database predicate level to wrong_session and wrong_ip metadata reasons, with a test pinning the where clause.

title: Extend isQueuedWcOrderPayload to validate externalOrderNumber and asOf
description: PR #165 tightened the pending FX queue type guard to validate reason, connector, externalOrderId (string), currency (string), and order.id (number). The PendingFxOrderPayload type also includes externalOrderNumber (string) and asOf (string | null) — neither is checked by isQueuedWcOrderPayload. The exported buildPendingFxOrderPayload always populates them, so production payloads from this code path are safe. The gap matters when a queue row bypasses the builder (data-migration insert, debug ops fixup, future second consumer). Tighten the guard to assert externalOrderNumber is a string and asOf is either null or a string so a malformed row at boundary time gets rejected rather than passed downstream.
acceptance_criteria: isQueuedWcOrderPayload in lib/connectors/woocommerce/sync/order-import.ts validates that externalOrderNumber is a string and asOf is either null or a string. tests/connectors/woocommerce-tax-fallback.test.ts adds rejection cases for {externalOrderNumber: 123} and {asOf: 0}.
close_reason: Fixed: pending WooCommerce FX queue payload guard now validates externalOrderNumber and asOf, with malformed payload regression cases.

title: Reconsider storing the full WcFullOrder in PendingFxOrderPayload (data residency)
description: PR #165 made the typed PendingFxOrderPayload include order: WcFullOrder explicitly. The shoppingSyncLog row in the pending FX queue therefore persists the full WC order snapshot — customer email, phone, billing/shipping addresses, line items, meta — until the queue is drained. This is pre-existing behavior (recordPendingFxOrder always wrote the full order), but the typed shape makes the data-residency footprint explicit. Operators only get notified when queue depth crosses WC_PENDING_FX_ORDER_NOTIFY_THRESHOLD (default 5), so the queue can sit with PII for unknown durations. Decide whether to (a) keep the full payload because the replay path genuinely needs all fields, (b) store a slimmer payload (e.g. just externalOrderId/currency/asOf and re-fetch the order from WC on replay), or (c) shorten the queue-row retention with an automated purge after a successful FX refresh.
acceptance_criteria: Decision recorded with rationale, plus either (a) a comment on PendingFxOrderPayload documenting why the full WcFullOrder is needed, (b) a slimmer payload type plus migration of existing rows, or (c) an automated purge with a configurable retention window.
close_reason: Fixed: decision recorded on PendingFxOrderPayload; pending FX retries intentionally persist the full WooCommerce order snapshot for deterministic replay, successful replay deletes queue rows, and normal shopping sync-log retention bounds failed rows.

title: Chunk batched tax-snapshot UPDATE ... FROM (VALUES ...) for large affected order sets
description: PR #164 replaced the per-parent UPDATE loop in lib/tax/document-tax-snapshot-refresh.ts with a single 'UPDATE sales_orders / purchase_orders ... FROM (VALUES (...), (...), ...)' batched statement per side. The parameterized query carries one row per affected parent. Tax-rate edits that touch tens or hundreds of draft orders work fine; tens-of-thousands of draft orders (mass-creation tenants, automated forecast PO generation, etc.) can push Postgres' parameterized-query size/binding limits in a single statement. Add chunked batching that processes deltas in groups of e.g. 1000 rows so the ceiling lifts predictably.
acceptance_criteria: updateSalesOrderTaxTotals and updatePurchaseOrderTaxTotals iterate the deltas map in fixed-size chunks (e.g. 1000) and execute one batched UPDATE per chunk. A test exercises a deltas Map larger than the chunk size and asserts that all affected orders receive the expected update.
close_reason: Fixed: tax snapshot parent-total updates now chunk large UPDATE ... FROM (VALUES ...) batches, with a >1000 parent regression test.

title: Add ExactKeys assertions for the remaining 3 finance empty-totals shapes (vat, apAging, fxGainLoss)
description: PR #162 added compile-time ExactKeys<> assertions for arAging and currencySummary, but the 3 other finance report types (vat, apAging, fxGainLoss) defined in FINANCE_ANALYTICS_EMPTY_TOTALS do not have the equivalent pairing test. The `satisfies` guard on each empty-totals constant only protects the empty values against their declared typed shape; it does NOT catch the typed shape drifting from the report function's actual `totals` shape. Without the pairing test, getVatReport could add a new totals key, VatReportTotals stays unchanged, and the empty-fallback returned by emptyFinanceAnalyticsReportForSourceLimit would silently be missing it. Add three more ExactKeys assertions to tests/app/analytics-page-utils.test.ts mirroring the arAging/currencySummary pattern.
acceptance_criteria: tests/app/analytics-page-utils.test.ts asserts at compile time that FINANCE_ANALYTICS_EMPTY_TOTALS.vat keys match Awaited<ReturnType<typeof getVatReport>>['totals'] keys (and similarly for apAging via getApAgingReport, fxGainLoss via getFxGainLossReport).
close_reason: Fixed: finance analytics empty totals now have ExactKeys compile-time assertions for VAT, AP aging, and FX gain/loss report totals.

title: Add row-cap rejection test for stock-position/inventory-ledger/inventory-costing CSV exports
description: PR #161 added tests that exercise the new get*ExportResponse functions in stock-position, inventory-ledger, and inventory-costing routes with mocked report data. The mocks return pageInfo.totalRows = 1, so the per-route rejectOversizedExport(50000-row cap) branch is never tested. Add a test per route that returns a mock with totalRows > STOCK_POSITION_CSV_ROW_LIMIT (and the equivalent constants for the other two routes) and asserts the response status is 413 Payload Too Large.
acceptance_criteria: tests/api/export-csv-metadata.test.ts (or a sibling file) has at least one row-cap rejection assertion per touched route. Each test mocks a report returning totalRows = 50001 and asserts response.status == 413.
close_reason: Fixed: CSV export route tests now assert oversized row caps for stock-position, inventory-ledger, and inventory-costing exports.

title: Add action-level integration test for updatePurchaseOrder FX re-resolution
description: PR #160 tests the resolver in isolation (tests/domain/purchasing/purchase-order-fx.test.ts) but does not exercise the full updatePurchaseOrder path that triggers the resolver. A regression that flipped where.fromCurrency/where.toCurrency, removed the fetchedAt: { lte } constraint, or skipped the re-resolve branch would not be caught. Add an integration-style test that: creates a draft PO at fxRateToBase=1 (base currency), changes currency to EUR with no fxRateToBase override and a stored FxRate, asserts the persisted fxRateToBase matches the stored rate.
acceptance_criteria: tests/domain/purchasing/ has a test (file or new module) that loads a fixture PO, calls updatePurchaseOrder with only currency changed, asserts the persisted fxRateToBase reflects a stored FxRate row at the validation moment.
close_reason: fixed

title: Document manual-refund replay asymmetry in createSalesOrderRefund
description: PR #159 added refund replay protection that only fires when input.externalRefundId is set, matching on (orderId, externalRefundId) and returning the persisted refund without side effects. Manual refunds have no replay protection at the service layer because manual refund submissions are guarded by operator UI flow. The asymmetry is intentional but not called out in the code.
acceptance_criteria: lib/domain/sales/refund-service.ts has a comment above the externalRefundId replay block stating that manual refunds intentionally skip replay protection and that operator-UI double-submit guards are the equivalent.
close_reason: Fixed: createSalesOrderRefund now documents that replay protection is intentionally scoped to external refund deliveries with stable externalRefundId keys, while manual refunds rely on operator UI double-submit guards rather than a synthetic service key.

title: Extract cancelPurchaseOrder business logic into lib/domain/purchasing/cancellation-service.ts
description: PR #159 added dependency-injection scaffolding to app/actions/purchase-orders.ts so cancelPurchaseOrder can be tested by injecting in-memory mocks. The deps-prop pattern works but adds cognitive load to an already-large action file. A cleaner shape is to lift the cancellation orchestration (load + transition + cost-layer reversal + accounting sync + activity log + stock sync) into lib/domain/purchasing/cancellation-service.ts, then have the action be a thin wrapper that does auth + revalidation. Tests target the domain service directly without dependency injection.
acceptance_criteria: Cancellation orchestration lives in a domain service module. cancelPurchaseOrder action body is < 30 lines (auth, call service, revalidate, return). CancelPurchaseOrderDeps type and defaultCancelPurchaseOrderDeps are removed or scoped to the service. Existing po-cancellation test suite continues to pass.
close_reason: Fixed: cancelPurchaseOrder orchestration moved into lib/domain/purchasing/cancellation-service.ts, the server action is now a thin auth/revalidate wrapper, action-level cancellation deps were removed, and the PO cancellation test targets the domain service directly.

title: Onboarding WC step doesn't surface wc_webhook_secret dual purpose (webhooks + invoice PDF handoff)
description:
PR #154 added a customer-facing shopping invoice PDF handoff route (`/api/shopping/[connector]/invoice-pdf`) that uses `wc_webhook_secret` as the HMAC signing key for outgoing requests from the WP helper plugin.

```ts
// lib/shopping-invoice-pdf.ts
const SHOPPING_INVOICE_SECRET_SETTING: Record<ShoppingConnectorId, string> = {
  woocommerce: 'wc_webhook_secret',
  shopify: 'shopify_webhook_secret',
}
```

The same secret now serves TWO purposes:
1. **Incoming**: WooCommerce → IMS webhook signature verification
2. **Outgoing**: WP helper plugin → IMS PDF request signature signing

This is a known cryptographic anti-pattern (filed as `onetwo3d-ims-d5l` during PR #154 review). The onboarding wizard sets `wc_webhook_secret` (via `saveShoppingConnectorCredentials` → settings store) but doesn't:

1. Explain that the secret has dual purpose
2. Reference the WP helper plugin setting page where the same value must be pasted
3. Warn about the rotation implications (rotating breaks BOTH webhook verification AND PDF download until WP plugin is updated)

**Operator-visible scenarios:**

- Operator sets WC webhook secret in IMS onboarding → forgets to set the same value in the WP helper plugin → customer-facing invoice PDF downloads start failing with HMAC signature mismatch
- Operator rotates WC webhook secret for security → unaware this breaks PDF requests until WP plugin is updated → invoice downloads silently fail for customers

**Suggested onboarding messaging:**

```
WooCommerce Webhook Secret
This secret is used for:
1. Verifying webhooks IMS receives from WooCommerce
2. Signing customer invoice PDF requests from the WooCommerce site to IMS

⚠️ Paste this same value in the OneTwoInventory Helper plugin
   settings page (WP admin → Settings → OneTwoInventory Helper).
   Rotating this secret requires updating it in BOTH places.
```

**Why this matters:**

The dual-purpose pattern was introduced silently in PR #154; operators upgrading from a previous deployment won't know about the new responsibility without documentation. New onboarding flows leave operators with a configuration trap.

Pair with the security improvement filed as `onetwo3d-ims-d5l` — if/when that issue is fixed by introducing a separate `wc_invoice_pdf_secret`, the onboarding messaging needs to reflect the split.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update WC connection settings section in onboarding (and /sync settings page) to explain the wc_webhook_secret dual purpose
- Add a sub-section pointing to the WP helper plugin's settings page with explicit 'paste the same value here' guidance
- Optional: surface the helper plugin download/install link from the WC settings page
- Coordinate with onetwo3d-ims-d5l (key separation) — if separate secrets are introduced, this onboarding text changes accordingly
notes: Filed during onboarding audit. Documentation gap rather than code defect. Lower priority but worth fixing alongside the WC onboarding refresh from the higher-priority connection-test-gate finding (mzs).
close_reason: Fixed: onboarding and /sync WooCommerce settings now document wc_webhook_secret reuse for incoming webhooks and OneTwoInventory Helper invoice PDF signing, including rotation implications.

title: Onboarding has no backup/cron configuration step — operators leave wizard without scheduled backups or cron secret
description:
Recent PRs added load-bearing backup + cron infrastructure that the onboarding wizard doesn't surface:

**PR #148 backup hardening:**
- New 50MB upload cap (`DATABASE_RESTORE_MAX_FILE_BYTES`)
- Backup manifest sidecar (auto-generated by cron backup)
- Disk-space preflight + restore session/IP binding
- Remote upload (S3/SFTP) for backup files

**PR #149 cron rate limits:**
- All cron routes now require `CRON_SECRET` + rate-limit token
- Cron registry expects per-job cadence (e.g., backup daily, fx-rates daily, invariant-check periodically)

**Onboarding wizard scope today:**
- Company / SMTP / public URL
- Currency / tax rates
- Integrations (WC / Xero / Shopify / Mintsoft)
- Product import

**Missing:**
- No step that prompts operator to schedule the backup cron, or verifies the cron secret is configured
- No step explaining backup is required for production deployment
- No setting for backup retention or remote-upload target
- No verification that `CRON_SECRET` is set in env (it's required for ALL cron routes per PR #149)

**Why this matters:**

Operator completes onboarding, starts using the system, and learns 6 months later when an issue happens that backups were never running. The cron infra is in place but the discoverability is zero from the wizard's perspective.

The wizard already verifies `publicAppUrl` and `smtp_host` etc. — adding a 'system health' or 'production readiness' step that checks:
- `CRON_SECRET` is set in env (server-side check)
- Scheduled backup cron has run at least once OR backup destination is configured
- FX rate cron has fetched rates within the last day (already shown elsewhere but worth surfacing here)

…would close the discoverability gap.

**Lower priority because:** this isn't a behavior regression — the wizard never had a backup step. The recent backup hardening adds value but operators completing the wizard before scheduling backups isn't worse than before. Worth filing as an opportunity rather than a defect.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a 'System Health & Production Readiness' step OR sub-section in the company step that checks:
  - CRON_SECRET env var presence
  - At least one scheduled backup run has succeeded (read from setting/activity log)
  - Backup remote target is configured (S3/SFTP) OR explicit 'local backup acknowledged'
  - Optional: invariant check has run successfully (PR #155 preflight)
- Surface a clear remediation if any check fails ('Add CRON_SECRET=... to your environment')
- Don't block wizard completion (warn but allow), since operators may be deploying without backups intentionally in dev
notes: Filed during onboarding audit. Wizard hasn't been updated for PR #148 / #149 / #155 infrastructure additions. Lower priority — opportunity rather than defect.
close_reason: Fixed: onboarding now surfaces production-readiness guidance for CRON_SECRET, scheduler application, scheduled backups, remote backup target, and operational health verification without blocking dev onboarding.

title: Sweep historical SELECT FOR UPDATE calls to use Prisma queryRaw
description: Several pre-existing domains still use Prisma  for SELECT ... FOR UPDATE row locks. PR #156 fixed the new product-lifecycle/preferred-supplier sites, but the older sales, WMS, transfer, purchasing, and connector call sites need a separate mechanical sweep with focused regression coverage because changing their transaction mocks and lock helpers cuts across multiple workflows.
close_reason: Fixed: converted historical SELECT ... FOR UPDATE row-lock reads from Prisma $executeRaw to $queryRaw across sales, transfers, manufacturing, purchasing, WMS/Mintsoft, refunds, shipments, and Xero/QuickBooks daily sync paths; advisory-lock executeRaw calls were left unchanged; added a source scanner regression test to prevent reintroducing executeRaw row-lock selects.

title: preferred-supplier.ts uses tx.$executeRaw for SELECT FOR UPDATE — should be $queryRaw (PR #156)
description:
PR #156's `lib/domain/purchasing/preferred-supplier.ts`:

```ts
await tx.$executeRaw(Prisma.sql`
  SELECT id
  FROM "products"
  WHERE id IN (${Prisma.join(productIds)})
  ORDER BY id ASC
  FOR UPDATE
`)
```

Prisma's `$executeRaw` is for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — it returns a row count. For SELECT, use `$queryRaw`. The current usage works incidentally because `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` is a side-effectful SELECT and Prisma doesn't reject the call shape, but:

1. `$executeRaw` returns `number` (row count) which is meaningless for a SELECT
2. Misleading to readers who expect `$executeRaw` to be a mutation
3. A future Prisma version might tighten the contract and reject `SELECT` via `$executeRaw`

This is the **third time** this anti-pattern appears in this codebase:
- PR #127 (PO cancellation cost-layer reversal) — same pattern in `po-cancellation.ts`, filed as a finding
- PR #156 (this PR, lifecycle archive helper) — same pattern in `product-lifecycle-archive.ts`:
  ```ts
  await tx.$executeRaw(Prisma.sql`SELECT id FROM "products" WHERE id = ${candidate.id} FOR UPDATE`)
  ```
- PR #156 (this PR, preferred-supplier helper) — this one

Worth a sweep across the codebase for `$executeRaw` + `SELECT.*FOR UPDATE` and replace with `$queryRaw` (or `$queryRawUnsafe` if the rule complains about parameter interpolation).

**Fix:**

```ts
await tx.$queryRaw(Prisma.sql`
  SELECT id
  FROM "products"
  WHERE id IN (${Prisma.join(productIds)})
  ORDER BY id ASC
  FOR UPDATE
`)
```

Same semantics, correct API.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replace $executeRaw with $queryRaw in preferred-supplier.ts SELECT FOR UPDATE
- Replace $executeRaw with $queryRaw in product-lifecycle-archive.ts SELECT FOR UPDATE (line ~88 of the new file)
- Grep for `$executeRaw` followed by `SELECT` across the codebase; fix any other instances
- Consider an ESLint rule to flag this pattern
notes: Filed during PR #156 review. Cosmetic / API hygiene; the SELECT FOR UPDATE locks work correctly. Worth a sweep so the pattern doesn't proliferate.
close_reason: Reconciled after importing origin/codex/dead-stock-report issues.jsonl: these PR #155/#156 findings were already fixed or superseded on the current PR branch before the import reopened their Beads state.

title: P8.2 'preserve forecast evidence on each line' needs schema spec — draft-PO lines have no metadata field today (PR #155 follow-up)
description:
PR #155 P8.2 acceptance:

> Generated draft PO lines carry enough forecast metadata to explain the suggested quantity.

But the existing `PurchaseOrderLine` schema doesn't have a structured place for forecast evidence:

- Existing fields: qty, unitCostForeign, unitCostBase, taxRateId, totalForeign, totalBase, taxBase, taxForeign, productId, poId, sortOrder, etc.
- No `forecastEvidence` or `reorderMetadata` field

The plan implies a schema addition without enumerating it. The 'forecast metadata' likely includes:
- Days-of-stock-remaining at forecast time
- Forecast horizon used (e.g., 30 days)
- Lead-time days
- Forecast model used (avg / median / EMA)
- Inputs (current stock, average daily sales, on-order qty, etc.)
- Generated-at timestamp

**Why this matters:**

The acceptance criterion 'lines carry enough forecast metadata to explain the suggested quantity' is unverifiable without knowing what 'enough' means and where it lives. Either:

1. **Add a JSON field to PurchaseOrderLine** for reorder metadata — e.g., `reorderEvidence Json? // null unless line was generated from a reorder forecast`. Schema migration needed.

2. **Add a parallel table** `ReorderDraftLineEvidence` keyed on the PO line — keeps the main schema clean but requires joins.

3. **Activity log only** — write a one-off activity log entry per line generated, with the forecast inputs. Less queryable but no schema change.

The plan doesn't pick. Without picking, the implementer guesses, and subsequent UI work to 'show the forecast evidence' depends on the guess.

**Also worth specifying:**

- What forecast model is used? Existing reorder report logic at `lib/domain/inventory/replenishment-reports.ts` — does P8.2 reuse this or introduce a new model?
- How is the suggested quantity computed? Days-of-stock × daily sales rate × safety factor? The plan should pin the formula or reference the existing one.
- The 'reorder-eligible' check: is it based on the existing reorder report's threshold logic, or a new rule?
acceptance_criteria:
- Update P8.2 to specify:
  - Where forecast evidence is stored (PO line field vs parallel table vs activity log)
  - What evidence is required (specific fields)
  - Whether P8.2 reuses the existing reorder-report logic or introduces new
  - Concrete forecast model / formula reference
notes: Filed during PR #155 second-pass review. P8.2 is plan-only; this is about pinning the schema decision before implementation begins.
close_reason: Reconciled after importing origin/codex/dead-stock-report issues.jsonl: these PR #155/#156 findings were already fixed or superseded on the current PR branch before the import reopened their Beads state.

title: invariant-check-preflight script: no integration test against the real runScheduledInvariantCheck path (PR #155)
description:
PR #155's tests (`tests/cron/invariant-check-preflight.test.ts`) pass a mocked `runCheck` function to `runInvariantCheckPreflight`:

```ts
const result = await runInvariantCheckPreflight(async () => scheduledResult({ ... }))
```

Four tests cover:
- Happy path (no critical findings)
- Critical findings present
- Summary/list divergence (defensive check)
- Partial report failure

What's NOT tested:

1. **The default `runCheck` parameter** (which constructs a real `runScheduledInvariantCheck` call with disabled side effects). The default is the codepath actually used by the script. If a future change breaks the parameter defaults (e.g., `writeActivityLog` accidentally re-enabled, causing CI side effects), no test catches it.

2. **The `scripts/invariant-check-preflight.ts` orchestration**: `disconnectDb`, `process.exit(process.exitCode ?? 0)`, console output format, MAX_PRINTED_CRITICAL_FINDINGS truncation. None of these have tests.

3. **End-to-end CI smoke**: the workflow yaml is verified-on-paper (run lint, type-check, validate). No test invokes `npm run invariant-check:preflight` against a known fixture and asserts behavior.

**Why this matters:**

The CI gate is binary (pass/fail) — its only output is the exit code. A regression that always passes (e.g., `preflight.ok` accidentally hard-coded to true, or the script's `process.exitCode = 1` not firing) would silently turn the gate into a no-op, and no test would catch it.

This is the same 'test by inspection, not behavior' theme as PRs #150 (`9pk`), #151 (`cgq`), #152 (helper-only), #153 (`40l`).

**Suggested fix:**

Add a focused script-level test that:
1. Sets up a fixture DB or a mock-injected dependency
2. Invokes the script's main function (extract it as exported for testability)
3. Asserts the exit code matches expectations for: clean run, critical findings, report errors
4. Asserts console output contains expected strings (truncation, remediation hint)
acceptance_criteria:
- Extract scripts/invariant-check-preflight.ts main() into a testable export
- Add tests for: exit-code 0 on clean, exit-code 1 on critical findings, exit-code 1 on report errors, MAX_PRINTED truncation
- Optionally: add an integration test that runs the real runScheduledInvariantCheck against a seeded fixture, asserts a known critical finding triggers exit 1
notes: Filed during PR #155 review. Recurring 'mocked test only' pattern across the rollout. Lower priority because the script is small and the failure modes are well-bounded.
close_reason: Reconciled after importing origin/codex/dead-stock-report issues.jsonl: these PR #155/#156 findings were already fixed or superseded on the current PR branch before the import reopened their Beads state.

title: P8.1 'incoming stock' definition for auto-archive job spans many tables — needs concrete query spec (PR #155)
description:
PR #155's P8.1 plan item proposes an automatic archive job:

> when an EOL product has zero stock in every warehouse and no incoming stock remains, transition it to archived
> Incoming stock should include open purchase-order receipts and any modeled inbound supply such as inbound transfers, manufacturing outputs, or WMS ASN/booked-in evidence.

'Incoming stock' as defined spans at least 5 different table contexts:

1. **Open PO lines**: `purchase_order_lines` with `qty - qtyReceived > 0` on POs in committed statuses (PO_SENT, SHIPPED, PARTIALLY_RECEIVED)
2. **Inbound stock transfers**: `stock_transfer_lines` with `qty - qtyReceived > 0` on transfers IN_TRANSIT to a warehouse holding the product
3. **Manufacturing output**: `production_orders` with status in pre-completion states where outputProductId matches
4. **WMS ASN evidence**: `wms_asn_lines` / `wms_booked_in_events` for the product
5. **Implicit inbound** — refund returns? Customer returns? Inbound from supplier returns?

A single 'has any incoming stock?' check requires querying ALL of these. The plan acceptance ('archive only after all warehouse stock is zero and incoming supply is zero') glosses over the actual implementation surface.

**Why this matters:**

1. Each table is independently maintained — missing one means false-positive archive (archive a product that has incoming stock the job didn't check)
2. The job needs to run periodically (cron) — frequent enough to catch the 'stock reached zero' transition, infrequent enough not to thrash. Plan doesn't specify cadence.
3. Race window: between the check and the status update, a new inbound supply event could land (e.g., operator creates a new PO line). The job needs to be transaction-safe.

**Suggested concrete spec for P8.1:**

Define a single `getProductIncomingStock(productId)` helper that:
- Returns a structured breakdown by source (open PO, transfer in, mfg output, WMS ASN, etc.)
- Each source is its own query function for testability
- Helper used by the auto-archive job AND by the operator-facing 'why didn't this archive?' diagnostic

Define cron cadence: probably daily, since the underlying event is 'stock dropped to zero', which itself happens at most a few times per day per product.

Define the race-safe transition: the archive should happen INSIDE a transaction that locks the product row and re-verifies the incoming-stock-zero condition.
acceptance_criteria:
- Update P8.1 in docs/plan.md with the concrete getProductIncomingStock breakdown (list of source tables and their conditions)
- Define cron cadence and rationale
- Define transaction-safety policy for the archive transition
- Add a test acceptance: 'job archives product P → no transitions happen if a new PO line for P is created between the read and the write'
notes: Filed during PR #155 review. Lower priority because P8.1 is plan-only; the implementer can fill in this detail. But it's the kind of detail that surfaces inconsistencies when not pinned upfront.
close_reason: Reconciled after importing origin/codex/dead-stock-report issues.jsonl: these PR #155/#156 findings were already fixed or superseded on the current PR branch before the import reopened their Beads state.

title: PR #154 reintroduces invoice- filename + 10-min TTL cap + weak password list — already-filed PR #146 findings recur (PR #154)
description:
PR #154 makes substantively the same changes to the IMS admin invoice-pdf flow that PR #146 made. Several findings on PR #146 recur in PR #154 verbatim:

- **Content-Disposition uses `invoice-${verification.payload.nonce}.pdf`** — random nonce in user's downloads folder, no security benefit. Filed as **onetwo3d-ims-2it** [P1].
- **DEFAULT_INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 10 * 60 = MAX_INVOICE_PDF_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS** — no admin override path. Filed as **onetwo3d-ims-quf** [P2].
- **`COMMON_PASSWORDS`** is a 7-entry hardcoded Set. Filed as **onetwo3d-ims-eda** [P2].
- **IP-binding via getClientIp() ?? 'unknown' fallback** — header-stripped requests collide on 'unknown'. Filed as **onetwo3d-ims-qv1** [P2] (PR #146) and **onetwo3d-ims-61b** [P2] (PR #148).
- **`wrong_session` / `wrong_ip` audit not surfaced separately** — security signal not aggregated. Filed as **onetwo3d-ims-d1f** [P3].

**Why this matters:** PR #146 was open at the time PR #154 was authored; PR #154 is presumably the merge-target that supersedes PR #146 with the additional shopping handoff route. The pre-existing findings carry forward and need to be addressed once at the merged scope, not separately per PR.

**No new work needed** — this issue exists to:
1. Flag the duplication so reviewers don't waste time re-finding the same issues
2. Link the PR #146 → PR #154 trail in the bd tracker
3. Make it clear which findings cluster needs to be addressed before PR #154 (or its merged successor) is merged
acceptance_criteria:
- When addressing PR #146 findings (or PR #154's equivalents), do so against whichever branch is the final merge target
- Add a 'duplicates: onetwo3d-ims-2it, quf, eda, qv1/61b, d1f' note to PR #154's bd row
- Close this issue when the underlying findings are addressed (or supersede with a single tracker if preferred)
notes: Filed during PR #154 review. Cross-reference for the recurring findings between PR #146 and PR #154.
close_reason: Closed as duplicate tracker: the repeated PR #146/#154 findings are now handled in their concrete issues. Filename (2it), max TTL (quf), password policy (eda), restore-token unknown IP (61b), and invoice IP-binding UX (qv1) are closed; remaining audit aggregation/dashboard work stays independently tracked by onetwo3d-ims-d1f.

title: getSidebarAnalyticsChildren exported from React component file — pulls React into test imports (PR #153)
acceptance_criteria:
- Extract getSidebarAnalyticsChildren, BASE_ANALYTICS_LINKS, REPORT_ACCESS_GROUPS, uniqueLinks, and SidebarLink type to lib/sidebar/analytics-access.ts (or similar)
- Update components/layout/sidebar.tsx to import from the new module
- Move tests/components/sidebar.test.ts to tests/lib/sidebar/analytics-access.test.ts and update import paths
- Confirm no other consumers of the sidebar file need updating
notes: Filed during PR #153 review. The refactor's stated goal is consolidation; this is just suggesting a more architectural consolidation pattern.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: sidebar analytics access was extracted to lib/sidebar/analytics-access.ts, the component imports it, and tests moved to tests/lib/sidebar/analytics-access.test.ts.

title: Sidebar tests assert hand-built href lists per role — couples test to data, not policy (PR #153)
acceptance_criteria:
- Refactor the test to assert the RULE (link appears iff canAccess returns true)
- Keep one happy-path snapshot test for ADMIN as a regression sanity check (catches widespread misconfiguration)
- Add a test asserting that BASE_ANALYTICS_LINKS appear only when role has 'analytics' permission (independently of the report groups)
- Add a test for the dedup behavior (currently uncovered): two groups with overlapping hrefs → assert the link appears exactly once
notes: Filed during PR #153 review. The current tests catch the happy path well; they're just expensive to maintain as the analytics surface grows.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: sidebar analytics tests now assert group permission rules, base analytics permission, admin snapshot, and link dedupe behavior.

title: Sidebar analytics gate behavior change: previously stock-position-only, now any-analytics-links (PR #153)
description:
PR #153 changes the Analytics NavGroup render gate:

```diff
- {canAccessStockPositionReports(userRole) && (
+ {analyticsChildren.length > 0 && (
    <NavGroup label="Analytics" icon={BarChart3} items={analyticsChildren} ... />
  )}
```

The old gate: show the Analytics group only if the role has stock-position access.
The new gate: show the Analytics group if ANY analytics link is accessible.

**For today's role definitions:** the test expected lists show every role with analytics links also has stock-on-hand → the behavior is identical in practice. ADMIN, MANAGER, FINANCE, READONLY all see Analytics; WAREHOUSE sees Analytics (because they have stock-position access); SUPPLIER sees nothing.

**For future role tuning:** if a role is granted 'analytics' permission OR access to one report group BUT has stock-position access removed:
- **Old**: Analytics group HIDDEN (gate failed on stock-position specifically)
- **New**: Analytics group SHOWN with that role's accessible links

This is a behavioral broadening with no permission policy change behind it.

The PR description says 'No auth policy change; this only consolidates the sidebar visibility logic'. The link contents are unchanged, but the gate condition's *intent* shifted from 'must have stock-position to see the group' to 'has any analytics link to see the group'. Worth confirming this aligns with intent.

**Why this matters:**

This is purely about future-proofing. Today, no role hits the edge case. The next time someone tweaks permissions (e.g., creates a 'SALES_ANALYST' role with only sales analytics access), the new gate decides correctly without intervention; the old gate would have required someone to remember to add stock-position permission. Could be a feature, could be a regression depending on intent.

**Possible improvements:**
- Document the gate's intent in a comment ('any analytics link → show the group') so it's clear this is deliberate
- Or, if the original 'requires stock-position' was intentional (e.g., 'admin gives stock-position as a sentinel for analytics visibility'), restore that semantic explicitly
acceptance_criteria:
- Confirm with the team: is the new 'any link visible → show group' gate intentional?
- If yes, add a code comment on the NavGroup render explaining the semantic
- Optionally: add a test asserting the gate-vs-content alignment (no role has analyticsChildren.length > 0 but is meant to hide the group)
notes: Filed during PR #153 review. The refactor is small and the behavior matches today, but the gate's semantic shifted in a way that's invisible until permissions are tuned.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: sidebar render gate is documented as visible-child driven and tests assert group visibility follows visible analytics links.

title: normalizeForFingerprint coerces undefined / null / '' to different hash inputs — accidental drift (PR #152)
description:
PR #152's normalizer:

```ts
function normalizeForFingerprint(value: unknown): unknown {
  if (value == null) return null   // null AND undefined → null (loose equality)
  if (value instanceof Date) return value.toISOString()
  if (Array.isArray(value)) return value.map(normalizeForFingerprint)
  if (typeof value === 'object') return Object.fromEntries(/* sorted keys */)
  return String(value)             // primitives → string
}
```

Edge cases:
- `{ url: undefined }` → normalized to `{ url: null }`
- `{ url: null }` → normalized to `{ url: null }`
- `{ url: '' }` → normalized to `{ url: '' }` (String('') = '')
- `{ url: 0 }` → normalized to `{ url: '0' }`
- `{ url: false }` → normalized to `{ url: 'false' }`

Two scenarios where this matters:

1. **Caller drift**: `buildEmailConnectionFingerprint` calls with `replyTo: data.reply_to.trim()`. If the user clears reply_to, `.trim()` yields `''` — normalized to `''` in fingerprint. But if some code path passes `undefined` (a refactor mistake, an optional field omitted), the fingerprint changes to include `null`. Same logical state, different hash → spurious gate failure.

2. **Refactor sensitivity**: a future change that adds a new optional input that's sometimes-set / sometimes-undefined silently produces different fingerprints in different code paths. The fingerprint stability across call sites is invisible without tests.

**Why this matters:** The fingerprint's purpose is to detect 'settings have meaningfully changed'. Empty-vs-undefined-vs-null distinctions are usually NOT meaningful changes from the user's perspective. The current normalizer half-collapses (null/undefined → null) but fully distinguishes empty strings from null. Inconsistent.

**Suggested policy:**
Treat all 'absent' values identically. Either:
- Strip undefined/null/'' keys before hashing (normalize-to-absent)
- Or coerce all to a sentinel like '' (normalize-to-empty)
- Document the policy in the helper's JSDoc
acceptance_criteria:
- Decide policy (drop-absent vs coerce-absent) and apply consistently
- Add tests asserting equivalent fingerprints for: `{ a: undefined }`, `{ a: null }`, `{}` (under the chosen policy)
- Add a test asserting `{ a: '' }` and `{}` produce the same fingerprint (or document why not)
- Audit all call sites for accidental missing fields that would silently change the hash
notes: Filed during PR #152 review. Lower priority — the existing call sites use `.trim()` consistently, so this is more about defensive robustness than a known bug.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: integration fingerprints drop undefined/null/empty-string values and tests assert absent values hash consistently.

title: X-IMS-Export-Metadata header needs Access-Control-Expose-Headers for fetch consumers (PR #151)
description:
PR #151 adds a custom HTTP response header `X-IMS-Export-Metadata` carrying export metadata as JSON.

For browser `fetch()` consumers calling these export routes:
- Same-origin requests can read the header without CORS configuration
- Cross-origin requests (e.g. an embedded dashboard, a future SDK, a third-party integration calling the export API) get the header in the response BUT cannot read it from JavaScript unless the server includes the custom header name in `Access-Control-Expose-Headers`

PR #151 doesn't:
- Add CORS configuration for this header
- Update any existing CORS middleware to expose it
- Document the CORS contract

For today's deployment (operator triggers download from within the dashboard same-origin), this is invisible. For future:
- Third-party reporting tools authenticating with API tokens and calling `/api/export/*` from a different origin → metadata silently inaccessible
- Embedded analytics widgets in a different parent frame → can't access metadata
- IMS SDK / client library → header is there, but most browsers strip it from `response.headers` because it's not in the exposed list

**Why this matters:** The PR closes P7.4 by relocating metadata from CSV rows to HTTP headers. The relocation is correct per the plan but the CORS implications mean the metadata is invisible to consumers that don't share origin. Today's use is fine; tomorrow's API surface needs CORS.
acceptance_criteria:
- Find the existing CORS middleware (if any) and add `X-IMS-Export-Metadata` to `Access-Control-Expose-Headers`
- If no middleware exists, document the same-origin-only contract in CSV_EXPORT_METADATA_HEADER's JSDoc
- Update docs/architecture.md to clarify: 'this metadata header is only accessible to same-origin fetch consumers; cross-origin clients require CORS exposure'
notes: Filed during PR #151 review. Lower priority because today's consumers are same-origin. File documented to surface when/if multi-origin CSV consumption becomes a use case.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: CSV export metadata headers are exposed through Access-Control-Expose-Headers, documented, and covered by export metadata tests.

title: No test coverage for loadFinanceAnalyticsReportForPage / loadPurchasingAnalyticsReportForPage wrapper behavior (PR #150)
description:
PR #150 adds page-level loader wrappers that catch `SourceScanTooLargeError` and return an empty report with a notice. The wrappers are deliverable features per the PR description: 'Handles source-scan limit failures at analytics page and CSV export boundaries with inline notices'.

The diff adds tests for:
- The underlying `SourceScanTooLargeError` throwing path (`tests/security/source-scan-error.test.ts`)
- The domain reports' assert-and-throw behavior (`tests/domain/finance/finance-period-analytics.test.ts` and friends)

But NOT for the wrappers themselves:
- `loadFinanceAnalyticsReportForPage`
- `loadPurchasingAnalyticsReportForPage`
- `loadSalesAnalyticsReportForPage`

These wrappers contain the catch logic and call the `empty*ReportForSourceLimit` helpers with the appropriate `emptyTotals`. A regression that:
- Catches the wrong error type (e.g., a broad `catch (e) {}`)
- Drops the notice from the returned empty report
- Returns null/undefined instead of an empty report
- Re-throws when it should return empty

…would not be caught by current tests.

**Why this matters:**

The wrappers are the user-visible contract: they're what decides whether a user sees a friendly notice or a 500 error page when they query too broadly. Regression-protection here is at least as important as the underlying throw.

Same gap pattern as PR #137's P1.7, PR #138's P4.4, PR #140's P3.1 — helper-level tests where action-level/integration-level tests are what the user actually relies on.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add tests for each of the three loader wrappers:
  - When the underlying load throws SourceScanTooLargeError: assert empty report returned, notice text is the error message, totals match the emptyTotals input
  - When the underlying load throws a different error: assert the error propagates
  - When the underlying load succeeds: assert the report passes through unchanged
- Consider DRY'ing the test cases across the three wrappers (they all share the same shape)
- File in tests/components or tests/page-utils to colocate with the wrappers
notes: Filed during PR #150 review. Recurring pattern in this rollout — file documented to consolidate the 'always test the integration layer too' theme.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: tests/app/analytics-page-utils.test.ts covers finance, sales, and purchasing page-loader SourceScanTooLargeError fallbacks, non-source-limit rethrow behavior, and empty totals.

title: InventoryHealthSourceLimitError extends SourceScanTooLargeError but redeclares limit and scanLabel — fragile inheritance (PR #150)
description:
PR #150 refactors `InventoryHealthSourceLimitError` to extend the new shared base:

```ts
export class InventoryHealthSourceLimitError extends SourceScanTooLargeError {
  readonly limit: number       // <-- shadows parent's readonly limit
  readonly scanLabel: string   // <-- parallel to parent's 'source' field

  constructor(limit: number, scanLabel: string) {
    super(`Inventory health ${scanLabel}`, limit, {
      message: `Inventory health ${scanLabel} exceeds ${limit.toLocaleString()} rows. ...`,
    })
    this.name = 'InventoryHealthSourceLimitError'
    this.limit = limit       // <-- writes to subclass's own field
    this.scanLabel = scanLabel
  }
}
```

Two problems:

1. **Field shadowing with `useDefineForClassFields`**: TypeScript class field declarations create the field on the instance. With class-fields semantics, the child's `readonly limit: number` declaration creates a new property descriptor AFTER `super()` returns, OVERWRITING whatever the parent's constructor set. Then `this.limit = limit` in the child reassigns. The end result is correct, but the inheritance is bypassing the parent's field initialization — a subtle code smell that future refactors of the parent class could break.

2. **`scanLabel` duplicates `source`**: the parent class already has a `source` field that holds essentially the same data. Reading code that uses `SourceScanTooLargeError` reads `.source`; code that knows about `InventoryHealthSourceLimitError` reads `.scanLabel`. Same logical concept, two names, two stored copies per instance.

**Why this matters:**

- Code that catches `SourceScanTooLargeError` (the new shared type) and reads `.source` gets the prefix-prefixed string (`Inventory health ${scanLabel}`).
- Code that catches the older `InventoryHealthSourceLimitError` reads `.scanLabel` for the unprefixed value.
- The two are NOT equivalent (one includes the prefix, the other doesn't).

Future caller might assume `error.source` equals `error.scanLabel` and conflate the two. The inheritance pattern conceals the divergence.
acceptance_criteria:
- Either:
  - **Option A**: drop the subclass entirely; have callers throw `SourceScanTooLargeError` directly with the prefixed source name. Update callers of `InventoryHealthSourceLimitError` to use `isSourceScanTooLargeError` instead.
  - **Option B**: keep subclass for naming, drop the duplicate fields; expose a getter `scanLabel` that strips the 'Inventory health ' prefix from parent's `source`.
- Pin behavior with a test asserting `error.source` and `error.scanLabel` either match or are documented to differ in a specific way
- Audit other places that might extend SourceScanTooLargeError for the same pattern (manufacturing-analytics likely)
notes: Filed during PR #150 review. The shared base is a clean refactor; the subclass-with-shadowing is the artifact of preserving backward compat. Worth cleaning up before more subclasses adopt the same pattern.
close_reason: Fixed InventoryHealthSourceLimitError to derive scanLabel from SourceScanTooLargeError.source without duplicate stored fields; added regression coverage.

title: endOfUtcDay uses 23:59:59.999 — comparisons miss microsecond-precision timestamps (PR #150)
description:
PR #150's shared `endOfUtcDay`:

```ts
export function endOfUtcDay(date: Date): Date {
  return new Date(Date.UTC(date.getUTCFullYear(), date.getUTCMonth(), date.getUTCDate(), 23, 59, 59, 999))
}
```

JS Dates have millisecond precision, but PostgreSQL `timestamp` and `timestamptz` columns store **microseconds** (0.001 ms). A query like:

```ts
where: { createdAt: { lte: endOfUtcDay(now) } }
```

…misses any row whose `createdAt` is in the last 0.999 milliseconds of the day (e.g. `2026-06-09 23:59:59.999500` UTC). PostgreSQL stores that microsecond-precision timestamp; the JS-generated bound is `23:59:59.999000`; `row.createdAt > endOfUtcDayMs` → excluded.

For a 24-hour window this is 0.999 / 86_400_000 = ~0.001% of timestamps. Sample size matters:
- 1 order per second → 1 missed row per 24h window (statistically)
- 100 orders per second → 100 missed rows per 24h window

**Why this matters:** For most analytics this is irrelevant noise. For VAT reports / period closings — where finance asserts "this period includes all transactions on or before this date" — even one missed transaction is a reconciliation discrepancy that finance has to chase.

**Standard fix:** use `< startOfNextDay` instead of `<= endOfDay`. The half-open interval cleanly captures every timestamp in the date.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replace `<= endOfUtcDay(d)` comparisons with `< startOfUtcDay(addUtcDays(d, 1))` (half-open interval)
- Or: deprecate `endOfUtcDay` and add `startOfNextUtcDay(d)` to the helper module
- Update analytic consumers to use the half-open pattern
- Add a test asserting that a row at `2026-06-09T23:59:59.9999Z` (or however PG returns sub-ms) is included in a query bounded by date 2026-06-09
- Document the precision caveat in the JSDoc of endOfUtcDay
notes: Filed during PR #150 review. Lower priority because the bug only matters at high throughput. But the PR consolidates the helper — fixing it once here fixes it everywhere downstream.
close_reason: Added startOfNextUtcDay and documented the endOfUtcDay PostgreSQL microsecond caveat; converted inventory ledger and negative-stock analytics filters to half-open lt next-day bounds with tests.

title: Cron rate limit DoS-by-exhaustion: any authenticated CRON_SECRET holder can lock out legitimate runs (PR #149)
description:
PR #149's `enforceCronRateLimit` keys on `cron:{jobName}` — a single global key per job, not per-IP.

Scenario:
1. CRON_SECRET is rotated / leaked (e.g., committed to a private repo, exposed in logs, exfiltrated from .env).
2. Attacker with the leaked secret sends 12 requests to `/api/cron/mintsoft-webhook-sweeper` in rapid succession.
3. All 12 pass `verifyCron` (valid secret).
4. All 12 consume the 12/hr quota.
5. Legitimate cron at the next scheduled tick is denied with 429.
6. For the next hour, the legitimate sweeper cannot run. Webhook events queue indefinitely.

The rate limit is meant as a second line of defense against accidental over-invocation. But in the leaked-secret threat model, it BECOMES an attack vector — quota exhaustion → legitimate workflow denial.

The current rate-limit primary mitigation (CRON_SECRET) is the primary defense. If that's already compromised, the rate-limit isn't preventing more harm — it's amplifying the harm (turning 12 attacker requests into 1+ hour of cron downtime).

**Why this matters:**
- PR #146 noted that token-binding has the same trade-off (security vs operational latency)
- The current key shape doesn't permit safe "rotate secret AND keep crons running" — operators have no way to clear the rate-limit counter mid-incident
- Distinguishing 'legitimate cron daemon IP' from 'attacker IP' would scope the quota correctly

**Lower priority because:** the threat requires CRON_SECRET leakage, which is the primary failure already.
acceptance_criteria:
- Key the rate limit on `cron:{jobName}:{sourceIp}` instead of `cron:{jobName}` — attacker IP exhausts its own slice, cron daemon's IP keeps working
- OR: add an admin-only endpoint to clear cron rate-limit counters for incident response
- OR: document the rotation procedure in docs/installation.md: 'After CRON_SECRET rotation, restart the rate-limit backend or wait 60min for the quota window to clear'
- Update lib/cron-rate-limit.ts JSDoc to acknowledge the trade-off
notes: Filed during PR #149 review. Defensive observation — not a blocker. The IP-keyed approach is cleanest but requires the same getClientIp handling flagged in PR #146/#148.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: cron rate-limit keys include verified source IPs, lib/cron-rate-limit.ts documents the leaked-secret tradeoff and rotation guidance, and tests/security/cron-rate-limit.test.ts covers IP-scoped quotas.

title: Split-batch Xero journals fragment finance's daily view — N journals per day instead of one (PR #149)
description:
PR #149's daily batch caps mean a day with >1000 of any entity (orders, shipments) gets split into multiple journal entries:

- 5000 paid orders for date X → 5 separate `DAILY_BATCH_REVENUE_DEFERRAL` journals over multiple cron runs
- Each journal has its own `referenceId` (`A1-2026-06-09-abc12345`, `A1-2026-06-09-def67890`, ...) and its own `reference` field (`Revenue Deferral 2026-06-09 abc12345`, `Revenue Deferral 2026-06-09 def67890`, ...)

Finance reading the Xero ledger sees 5 'Revenue Deferral 2026-06-09 {hash}' entries instead of 1. Reconciliation requires summing all 5 to match the IMS daily total. The hash suffix is opaque to finance — they have to manually correlate.

**Why this matters:** The pre-PR behavior was 1 journal per day per group. Tenants with small volumes (<1000/day) keep that. Tenants with larger volumes get a different reporting structure than smaller tenants. The change in journal shape isn't documented in CHANGELOG and finance teams will see this on the first overflow day with no warning.

The split is operationally necessary (single 5000-line journals are slow and fragile in Xero), but the UX of multiple per-day journals is a downstream concern that wasn't addressed.

Possible mitigations (each has trade-offs):

1. **Aggregate at month-end**: split journals retained for audit trail but a reconciliation report rolls up by date
2. **Use Xero 'manual journal grouping'** if it supports linking multiple journals as one logical posting
3. **Document the new structure**: add a CHANGELOG entry explaining the journal split for tenants > 1000 daily orders, with guidance on reconciliation
4. **Use a shared correlation field** that finance can filter on (a 'batchDate' tag or similar)
acceptance_criteria:
- Add CHANGELOG.md entry: 'For tenants posting more than 1000 orders/shipments per day, daily batch journals are now split into multiple entries per day. Each entry's reference includes a deterministic hash; reconciliation should sum entries matching the date prefix.'
- Add a reference column / metadata field to the Xero manual journal payload that finance can use to group: e.g., `batchDate: '2026-06-09'` separate from the `reference` text field
- Consider a settings-page report that shows 'Daily batch summary for date X' aggregating across all split batches — operator-friendly reconciliation view
- Acceptance criteria for the docs/plan.md plan group 15 completion should include 'finance reconciliation guidance documented'
notes: Filed during PR #149 review. Lower priority than gnt because it's UX/finance-team friction rather than data correctness. But it's the first thing finance will complain about.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: CHANGELOG and docs/architecture document split daily-batch journals, and Xero daily-sync payloads include batchDate, batchGroup, batchReferenceId, and splitBatch metadata for reconciliation.

title: PDF token audit treats wrong_session and wrong_ip as ordinary verification failures — security signal lost (PR #146)
description:
PR #146 adds new `wrong_session` and `wrong_ip` reasons to `PdfTokenVerificationFailureReason`. The audit logger (`auditInvoicePdfTokenAttempt`) treats them the same as any other verification failure — they get logged but not distinguished as potential token-sharing / theft signals.

These two failure modes are interesting because they specifically indicate:
- `wrong_session`: a valid token from a previous auth session is being presented. Could be a re-authenticated user using a bookmarked link (benign) or a stolen token (malicious).
- `wrong_ip`: a token signed for IP A is being presented from IP B. Could be a network change (benign per `onetwo3d-ims-qv1`) or a token shared/leaked (suspicious).

Aggregated patterns matter:
- `wrong_session` for the SAME user repeatedly → likely benign (user re-authed)
- `wrong_ip` from many different source IPs for tokens belonging to the SAME user → token shared in a public Slack channel
- `wrong_session` + `wrong_ip` from a different user agent → likely stolen

Current audit logs each attempt at the same level, with no aggregation, no rate-based alerting.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a separate higher-severity activity log entry (`level: 'WARNING'` instead of `'INFO'`) for `wrong_session` and `wrong_ip` reasons
- Include user-agent in the audit row metadata so investigators can spot UA mismatches
- Add a dashboard / settings widget that surfaces recent `wrong_session` / `wrong_ip` events grouped by invoice id, similar to the tax-rate fallback widget added in PR #144
- (Optional) Add a rate-based alert: if more than N `wrong_*` events for the same invoice id in T minutes, notify admins
notes: Filed during PR #146 review. Lower priority because the audit logging happens; this is about extracting more value from it. Operational maturity, not correctness.
close_reason: Fixed: invoice PDF wrong_session/wrong_ip failures now use a distinct invoice_pdf_token_security_signal activity action, preserve WARNING level and user-agent metadata, and Sales Settings surfaces recent grouped security signals by sales order with focused tests.

title: Plan items newly marked Complete in PR #145 lack test pointers / verifying-commit refs (P0.1-P0.5, P2.1, P2.2, P2.4)
description:
Codex adversarial review of PR #145 identified:

Multiple `Status: Complete` markers were added in this PR's `docs/plan.md` changes (P0.1–P0.5, P2.1, P2.2, P2.4) without:
1. Replacing the future-tense test text ("Add an integration test...") with an actual test file path
2. Including a `Reference:` line pointing to the implementing/verifying commit SHA

This matches the "Complete marker rests on inspection" anti-pattern called out earlier in this rollout (see bd onetwo3d-ims-8c7 for the P1.3/P1.5/P4.2 sweep).

**Why this matters:** The plan.md is the canonical audit document for the production-readiness rollout. "Complete" markers backed only by PR-body assertions (rather than test pointers) are unverifiable by future contributors. Three months from now, someone auditing the plan won't know whether P0.1 was actually fixed or just claimed-fixed.

**Concrete findings from PR #145's docs/plan.md hunk:**
- P0.1 marked Complete — original Tests line still says "Add an integration test that hits /api/auth/totp-setup..."
- P0.2 marked Complete — original Tests line still future-tense
- P0.3, P0.4, P0.5 — same pattern
- P2.1, P2.2, P2.4 — same pattern
acceptance_criteria:
- For each newly-marked-complete item, either:
  - Replace the future-tense Tests line with the actual test file path + test name (e.g. `tests/security/totp-setup-route.test.ts::"rejects when TOTP already configured"`), OR
  - Add a `Reference:` line naming the implementing commit SHA + brief verification note
- Audit the consolidated section's checkbox list to confirm every checked item also has a pointer
- Same sweep needed for PR #143's bd-issue closures (hyz, a8u, ahu) — those PRs closed bd issues with prose close_reasons but the plan.md entries may not have test pointers
notes: Filed during Codex review of PR #145. Codex severity P3 (process/cosmetic) — the markers don't break code, but they degrade the audit value of the plan.md document. Related: bd onetwo3d-ims-8c7 covers the same sweep for P1.3/P1.5/P4.2.
close_reason: Fixed/audited docs/plan.md complete entries: P0.1-P0.5 and P2.1/P2.2/P2.4 now point at concrete tests or validation commands instead of future-tense placeholders.

title: Cost-layer revaluation audit log stores previous + patched + delta — 3x storage overhead (PR #144)
description:
PR #144's `recordCostLayerSnapshotRevaluation()` (lib/cost-layers.ts) writes an activity log row per snapshot revaluation with metadata containing:

```ts
metadata: {
  ...
  changedEntries: params.changedEntries,    // entries that changed
  previousSnapshot: params.previousSnapshot,  // FULL old snapshot
  patchedSnapshot: params.patchedSnapshot,    // FULL new snapshot
}
```

For a ShipmentLine with 50 cost-layer entries where 1 entry changes, the activity log row stores:
- 1 changedEntry (the delta)
- 50 entries in previousSnapshot
- 50 entries in patchedSnapshot

Same data stored ~3x. For a landed-cost adjustment touching 100 shipment lines, that's ~300x the snapshot data in activity_logs.

**Why this matters:** The original bd issue `onetwo3d-ims-imn` (now closed by this PR) asked for an audit trail — but didn't specify the audit needed to store the full snapshot. The changedEntries field alone reconstructs the delta. Both full snapshots are operationally useful for one-off forensics but bloat the activity_logs table for routine landed-cost revaluations.

activity_logs is a hot table (other reports, dashboard queries, daily aggregations read it). Bloat here propagates.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decide retention/granularity trade-off:
  - Option A: store only changedEntries (the delta) — reconstruct full snapshots from delta + current shipment_line.costLayerSnapshot on demand
  - Option B: store previousSnapshot only (since patchedSnapshot equals shipment_line.costLayerSnapshot after the update)
  - Option C: keep current (3x) but document the trade-off and add a sweeper that compacts old revaluation rows after N days
- Add a row-size assertion test that proves a 50-entry snapshot doesn't produce a 50KB activity_log row
- If retention is bounded (e.g., 90 days), this becomes self-limiting and current shape is fine — but should be explicit
notes: Identified during PR #144 review. Lower priority because storage is cheap and audit trails are valuable — but worth knowing before the activity_logs table hits a size that affects query performance.
close_reason: Already satisfied on development: recordCostLayerSnapshotRevaluation stores changedEntries plus entry counts only, not full previous/patched snapshots; tests assert the compact activity-log metadata shape.

title: Clean up stale test path pointers in docs/plan.md (P1.3, P1.5, P4.2)
description:
Several `**Status:** Complete` plan items in docs/plan.md retained their original `**Tests:**` lines, which point at file paths that don't exist on origin/development.

Stale pointers found during PR #140 review audit:

| Item | Plan claims | Actually exists |
|---|---|---|
| P1.3 | `tests/refund-service.idempotency.test.ts` | Path doesn't exist. Adjacent helper-level tests live in `tests/domain/sales/refund-service.test.ts` and `tests/woocommerce-refund-sync.test.ts`. |
| P1.5 | `tests/refund-service.revaluation.test.ts` | Path doesn't exist. Revaluation tests are in `tests/domain/sales/refund-service.test.ts` (lines 703, 759, 815). |
| P4.2 | 'Complete two production orders same day; assert FIFO order matches completedAt' (no file path) | No such integration test. Helper-level coverage only in `tests/domain/manufacturing/manufacturing-action-inputs.test.ts`. |

**Why this matters:** Anyone auditing the plan to verify a completed item follows a broken link, gets nothing, and either re-implements the test (waste) or assumes coverage exists (silent regression risk).
acceptance_criteria:
- Update docs/plan.md P1.3 Tests line to point at actual test file + test name
- Update docs/plan.md P1.5 Tests line to point at the three landed cost revaluation tests in refund-service.test.ts
- Either fix P4.2 Tests line (after the P4.2 follow-up issue lands integration test) OR remove the prose acceptance criterion and replace with reference to the helper-level test
- Audit remaining `**Status:** Complete` items for similar stale pointers
notes: Two follow-up issues filed for P1.3 and P4.4 missing integration tests; this issue is just the docs cleanup. P1.5's tests do exist, just in a different file than the plan claims — fix the pointer.
close_reason: Fixed docs/plan.md stale pointers: P1.5 now references actual refund-service revaluation tests, and P4.2 now references existing manufacturing receivedAt helper coverage while pointing to z0g for the remaining same-day FIFO integration follow-up.

title: Compound tax / reverse charge / EC sales / OSS not supported
description:
Each line carries a single taxRateId/taxRatePercent. Use cases not currently expressible:
- Compound taxes (e.g. VAT + provincial surcharge)
- Reverse charge B2B (zero-rated outbound, customer self-accounts)
- EC sales / OSS reporting buckets
- Multi-tax stacking for jurisdictions with both federal and state tax

Lower priority than the per-line fallback bugs (xx4, hyz) but worth tracking. UK-only sellers won't notice; expansion to EU OSS or the US would expose this.

Fix direction:
- Schema: a join table linking SalesOrderLine/PurchaseOrderLine to multiple TaxRate rows with order/compound flags.
- Resolver: extend pickTaxRate to return a list of applicable rates with compound rules.
- Connector mapping: confirm Xero TaxComponent / QB ItemBased tax codes can carry compound rates.
close_reason: Implemented tax rate component profiles with compound, reverse-charge, and reporting metadata while preserving existing document line tax snapshots.

title: Native VAT / tax liability report (currently delegated to Xero/QB)
description:
